summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Levels of Organization (Cells → Tissues → Organs → Systems)
summary_sections.content_html:
Levels of Structural Organization

The human body is organized from the smallest to largest levels:

  • Chemical level: Atoms combine into molecules (e.g., H₂O, DNA).
  • Cellular level: Cells are the basic units of life (e.g., neurons, muscle cells).
  • Tissue level: Groups of similar cells (4 types: epithelial, connective, muscle, nervous).
  • Organ level: Two or more tissues form a functional unit (e.g., heart, liver).
  • Organ system level: Organs work together (11 major systems).
  • Organismal level: All systems cooperate to maintain life.

flashcards.definition: Atoms and molecules; the simplest structural level of the body.
The basic living unit of all organisms; performs all life functions.
A group of similar cells performing a common function (4 types).
A structure composed of two or more tissue types that performs a specific function.
A group of organs that work together to accomplish a common purpose (e.g., cardiovascular system).

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Anatomical Terminology (Planes, Directions, Cavities)
summary_sections.content_html:
Anatomical Terminology

Anatomical Position

Standing erect, feet forward, palms facing anteriorly. All directional terms reference this position.

Directional Terms

  • Superior/Inferior: Above/below.
  • Anterior (ventral)/Posterior (dorsal): Front/back.
  • Medial/Lateral: Toward/away from the midline.
  • Proximal/Distal: Closer/farther from the trunk (for limbs).
  • Superficial/Deep: Toward/away from the body surface.

Body Planes

  • Sagittal: Divides left/right. Midsagittal = equal halves.
  • Frontal (coronal): Divides anterior/posterior.
  • Transverse (horizontal): Divides superior/inferior (cross-section).

Body Cavities

Dorsal cavity (cranial + vertebral) and ventral cavity (thoracic + abdominopelvic).

flashcards.definition: Standing erect, feet forward, arms at sides, palms facing anteriorly.
Divides the body into left and right portions; midsagittal = equal halves.
Divides the body into anterior (front) and posterior (back) portions.
Divides the body into superior (upper) and inferior (lower) portions.
Medial = toward the midline; lateral = away from the midline.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Homeostasis & Feedback Mechanisms
summary_sections.content_html:
Homeostasis & Feedback

Homeostasis: The body's ability to maintain a relatively stable internal environment despite external changes.

Components of a Feedback Loop

  • Receptor (sensor): Detects a stimulus/change.
  • Control center (integrator): Processes the signal, determines the response (often the brain).
  • Effector: Carries out the response (muscles, glands).

Types

  • Negative feedback: Reverses the change (most common). Example: thermoregulation.
  • Positive feedback: Amplifies the change until the process completes. Example: childbirth (oxytocin).

flashcards.definition: Maintenance of a stable internal environment (temperature, pH, blood glucose, etc.).
A response that reverses (reduces) the initial stimulus; most common feedback type.
A response that amplifies the stimulus until the process is complete.
The sensor that detects a change in the internal or external environment.
The organ/tissue that carries out the corrective response (e.g., muscle, gland).

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Basic Histology (Tissue Types)
summary_sections.content_html:
Basic Histology — The Four Tissue Types

1. Epithelial Tissue

  • Covers body surfaces, lines cavities, forms glands.
  • Classified by layers (simple vs. stratified) and cell shape (squamous, cuboidal, columnar).

2. Connective Tissue

  • Supports, protects, binds. Contains cells in an extracellular matrix.
  • Types: loose (areolar), dense (tendons), cartilage, bone, blood.

3. Muscle Tissue

  • Generates force. Three types: skeletal (voluntary, striated), cardiac (involuntary, striated), smooth (involuntary, nonstriated).

4. Nervous Tissue

  • Transmits electrical signals. Neurons + supporting neuroglia (glial cells).

flashcards.definition: Lines and covers surfaces; classified by cell shape and layer number.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Medical Imaging Techniques
summary_sections.content_html:
Medical Imaging Techniques

X-Ray

Uses ionizing radiation. Best for bones and detecting fractures. Low cost, fast.

CT (Computed Tomography)

Multiple X-ray images processed by computer to create cross-sectional slices. Good for soft tissue and bone.

MRI (Magnetic Resonance Imaging)

Uses strong magnets and radio waves. Superior soft-tissue contrast. No ionizing radiation.

Ultrasound

Uses sound waves. No radiation. Common for fetal imaging, heart (echocardiogram), and abdominal organs.

PET (Positron Emission Tomography)

Uses radioactive tracers to detect metabolic activity. Used in cancer diagnosis and brain studies.

flashcards.definition: Ionizing radiation technique; best for bone structures and fractures.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Case Studies in Homeostasis
summary_sections.content_html:
Case Studies in Homeostasis

Case 1 — Dehydration

A runner loses excessive water through sweat. Osmoreceptors detect increased blood osmolarity → hypothalamus signals ADH release → kidneys reabsorb more water → blood osmolarity returns to normal.

Case 2 — Diabetes (Type 1)

Autoimmune destruction of beta cells → no insulin → blood glucose stays elevated → homeostatic failure without treatment.

Case 3 — Heatstroke

Body's cooling mechanisms overwhelmed → core temp rises above 40°C → positive feedback cycle (enzyme dysfunction worsens temp control) → medical emergency.

flashcards.definition: Excess water loss triggers ADH release, increasing kidney water reabsorption.
Released by posterior pituitary; causes kidneys to reabsorb water.

Failure of thermoregulatory homeostasis; core temp > 40°C; medical emergency.
When the body cannot maintain stable conditions; leads to disease or death.

summary_sections.content_html:
AP Prep: Anatomical Terminology

This lesson reviews high-yield anatomical terms and concepts that frequently appear on standardized exams.

Key AP Review Points

  • Know all directional terms relative to anatomical position.
  • Identify body regions: cranial, cervical, thoracic, abdominal, pelvic, upper/lower extremities.
  • Serous membranes: parietal (lines wall), visceral (covers organ). Examples: pleura, pericardium, peritoneum.
  • Abdominopelvic quadrants: RUQ, LUQ, RLQ, LLQ.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Bone Structure & Function
summary_sections.content_html:
Bone Structure & Function

Functions of Bone

  • Support, protection, movement (lever for muscles), mineral storage (Ca²⁺, PO₄³⁻), blood cell formation (hematopoiesis), energy storage (yellow marrow fat).

Gross Anatomy of a Long Bone

  • Diaphysis: Shaft; compact bone surrounding the medullary cavity (yellow marrow).
  • Epiphysis: Ends; spongy (cancellous) bone covered by articular cartilage.
  • Epiphyseal plate/line: Growth plate (cartilage in children; ossified line in adults).
  • Periosteum: Outer fibrous membrane covering bone; rich in blood vessels and nerves.
  • Endosteum: Thin membrane lining the medullary cavity.

flashcards.definition: The shaft of a long bone; composed of compact bone surrounding the medullary cavity.
The end of a long bone; contains spongy bone and articular cartilage at the joint surface.
Dense fibrous membrane covering the outer surface of bone (except at joints).

Growth plate made of cartilage; allows bones to lengthen during development.

u2_l2.2.title: Types of Bones (Long, Short, Flat, Irregular)

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Types of Bones (Long, Short, Flat, Irregular)
summary_sections.content_html:
Classification of Bones by Shape

  • Long bones: Longer than wide; shaft + two ends. Examples: femur, humerus, phalanges.
  • Short bones: Roughly cube-shaped. Examples: carpals (wrist), tarsals (ankle).
  • Flat bones: Thin, flattened, often curved. Examples: skull bones, sternum, scapula, ribs.
  • Irregular bones: Complex shapes. Examples: vertebrae, hip bones, some facial bones.
  • Sesamoid bones: Embedded in tendons. Example: patella (kneecap).

flashcards.definition: Longer than wide, with a shaft and two ends (e.g., femur, humerus).
Cube-shaped; found in wrist (carpals) and ankle (tarsals).
Thin, flattened, often curved; provides protection and broad surfaces (e.g., skull, sternum).

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Axial Skeleton
summary_sections.content_html:
The Axial Skeleton

The axial skeleton (80 bones) forms the central axis: skull, vertebral column, thoracic cage.

Skull (22 bones)

  • Cranial bones (8): frontal, parietal (2), temporal (2), occipital, sphenoid, ethmoid.
  • Facial bones (14): mandible, maxilla (2), zygomatic (2), nasal (2), lacrimal (2), palatine (2), inferior nasal conchae (2), vomer.

Vertebral Column (26 bones)

  • Cervical (7), Thoracic (12), Lumbar (5), Sacrum (1, fused 5), Coccyx (1, fused 3–5).

Thoracic Cage

  • Sternum (manubrium, body, xiphoid), 12 pairs of ribs: true (1–7), false (8–10), floating (11–12).

flashcards.definition: 80 bones forming the body's central axis: skull, vertebral column, bony thorax.
7 neck vertebrae (C1–C7); C1 = atlas, C2 = axis.
12 vertebrae (T1–T12) articulating with ribs.
5 lower back vertebrae (L1–L5); largest and strongest.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Appendicular Skeleton
summary_sections.content_html:
The Appendicular Skeleton

126 bones of the limbs and girdles that attach them to the axial skeleton.

Pectoral (Shoulder) Girdle

  • Clavicle (collarbone) + scapula (shoulder blade) on each side.

Upper Limb (30 bones each side)

  • Humerus, radius, ulna, 8 carpals, 5 metacarpals, 14 phalanges.

Pelvic Girdle

  • Each hip bone (os coxae) = ilium + ischium + pubis (fused). Two hip bones + sacrum = pelvis.

Lower Limb (30 bones each side)

  • Femur, patella, tibia, fibula, 7 tarsals, 5 metatarsals, 14 phalanges.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Joints & Movement
summary_sections.content_html:
Joints (Articulations) & Movement

Classification by Structure

  • Fibrous: No joint cavity; bones joined by fibers (e.g., skull sutures).
  • Cartilaginous: Bones joined by cartilage (e.g., intervertebral discs, pubic symphysis).
  • Synovial: Joint cavity with synovial fluid; most movable (e.g., knee, shoulder, hip).

Types of Synovial Joints

  • Plane (gliding), Hinge (elbow), Pivot (atlas/axis), Condyloid (wrist), Saddle (thumb), Ball-and-socket (hip, shoulder).

Movements

Flexion/extension, abduction/adduction, rotation, circumduction, pronation/supination.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Bone Growth & Remodeling
summary_sections.content_html:
Bone Growth & Remodeling

Ossification

  • Intramembranous: Bone forms directly from mesenchyme (flat bones of skull).
  • Endochondral: Bone replaces a hyaline cartilage model (most bones).

Long Bone Growth

  • Length: Epiphyseal plate cartilage proliferates → ossifies progressively.
  • Width: Appositional growth — osteoblasts add bone to the periosteal surface.

Remodeling

Continuous process: osteoclasts resorb old bone, osteoblasts deposit new bone. Regulated by hormones (PTH, calcitonin) and mechanical stress (Wolff's law).

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Disorders of the Skeletal System
summary_sections.content_html:
Disorders of the Skeletal System

Common Conditions

  • Osteoporosis: Bone mass loss → fragile bones; common in postmenopausal women.
  • Osteoarthritis (OA): Wear-and-tear degeneration of articular cartilage.
  • Rheumatoid Arthritis (RA): Autoimmune destruction of joint synovial membranes.
  • Fractures: Complete/incomplete, open/closed, stress, pathological.
  • Osteogenesis imperfecta: Genetic disorder; brittle bones (collagen defect).
  • Paget's disease: Excessive/disorganized bone remodeling.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Muscle Tissue Types (Skeletal, Cardiac, Smooth)
summary_sections.content_html:
Three Types of Muscle Tissue

  • Skeletal muscle: Voluntary, striated, multinucleated; attached to bones for movement.
  • Cardiac muscle: Involuntary, striated, branching cells with intercalated discs; found only in the heart.
  • Smooth muscle: Involuntary, nonstriated, spindle-shaped; found in walls of hollow organs (GI tract, blood vessels, bladder).

Key Differences

Skeletal is the only type under conscious control. Cardiac muscle is self-exciting (autorhythmicity). Smooth muscle sustains long, slow contractions.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Muscle Anatomy & Physiology
summary_sections.content_html:
Skeletal Muscle Anatomy

Organizational Hierarchy

  • Muscle → Fascicle → Muscle fiber (cell) → Myofibril → Sarcomere
  • Each muscle fiber is a single multinucleated cell wrapped in sarcolemma (cell membrane).
  • Sarcoplasmic reticulum (SR) stores Ca²⁺ ions.

Connective Tissue Wrappings

  • Endomysium: Around each fiber. Perimysium: Around each fascicle. Epimysium: Around the entire muscle.

Neuromuscular Junction (NMJ)

Motor neuron meets muscle fiber → acetylcholine (ACh) released → triggers contraction.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Sliding Filament Theory
summary_sections.content_html:
The Sliding Filament Theory

Muscle contraction occurs when thin filaments (actin) slide over thick filaments (myosin), shortening the sarcomere.

Steps of the Cross-Bridge Cycle

  • 1. Ca²⁺ released from SR binds to troponin → tropomyosin shifts → exposes binding sites on actin.
  • 2. Myosin head (already energized by ATP hydrolysis) binds actin → cross-bridge forms.
  • 3. Power stroke: myosin head pivots, pulling actin toward the center (ADP + Pᵢ released).
  • 4. New ATP binds myosin → detachment from actin.
  • 5. ATP hydrolyzed → myosin re-cocks. Cycle repeats as long as Ca²⁺ is present.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Muscle Contraction & Energy Use
summary_sections.content_html:
Muscle Contraction & Energy Systems

Types of Contraction

  • Isotonic: Muscle changes length (concentric = shortening, eccentric = lengthening).
  • Isometric: Muscle generates force without changing length (holding a weight still).

Energy Sources

  • Immediate: ATP (2–3 sec) → Creatine phosphate (5–8 sec).
  • Short-term: Anaerobic glycolysis (30–60 sec) — glucose → lactic acid + 2 ATP.
  • Long-term: Aerobic (oxidative) metabolism — glucose/fat + O₂ → 36 ATP (sustains long activity).

Muscle Fatigue

Decline in force due to ATP depletion, lactic acid buildup, and electrolyte imbalance.

flashcards.definition: Muscle changes length; concentric (shortens) or eccentric (lengthens under load).
Muscle generates force without changing length (e.g., holding a wall push).
Immediate energy reserve; donates phosphate to ADP → ATP (lasts ~8 seconds).
Glucose broken down without oxygen; yields 2 ATP + lactic acid.
O₂-dependent breakdown of glucose/fat; yields ~36 ATP per glucose.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Major Muscle Groups
summary_sections.content_html:
Major Muscle Groups

Upper Body

  • Deltoid: Shoulder abduction. Biceps brachii: Elbow flexion/forearm supination. Triceps brachii: Elbow extension.
  • Pectoralis major: Arm flexion/adduction/medial rotation. Latissimus dorsi: Arm extension/adduction.

Core

  • Rectus abdominis: Trunk flexion. External/internal obliques: Trunk rotation.
  • Erector spinae: Back extension and posture.

Lower Body

  • Quadriceps femoris: Knee extension. Hamstrings: Knee flexion/hip extension. Gluteus maximus: Hip extension. Gastrocnemius: Plantar flexion (calf).

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Disorders of the Muscular System
summary_sections.content_html:
Disorders of the Muscular System

  • Muscular dystrophy (MD): Genetic; progressive muscle degeneration. Duchenne MD is the most common childhood form (dystrophin gene mutation).
  • Myasthenia gravis: Autoimmune; antibodies attack ACh receptors at the NMJ → progressive weakness.
  • Fibromyalgia: Chronic widespread muscle pain; cause unknown.
  • Rhabdomyolysis: Rapid muscle breakdown releasing myoglobin → kidney damage.
  • Strains: Overstretched or torn muscle fibers (different from sprains, which involve ligaments).

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Case Studies in Sports Medicine
summary_sections.content_html:
Case Studies in Sports Medicine

Case 1: ACL Tear

A basketball player lands awkwardly → knee gives out. Diagnosis: Anterior cruciate ligament (ACL) tear. Treatment: surgery (graft) + physical therapy rehabilitation.

Case 2: Rotator Cuff Injury

A swimmer experiences shoulder pain with overhead motion. Rotator cuff muscles (supraspinatus, infraspinatus, teres minor, subscapularis) stabilize the shoulder.

Case 3: Achilles Tendon Rupture

Sudden calf pain during sprinting → unable to plantar flex. The Achilles tendon (calcaneal tendon) connects gastrocnemius/soleus to the calcaneus.

flashcards.definition: Key knee stabilizer preventing anterior tibial translation; common sports injury.
Four muscles (SITS: supraspinatus, infraspinatus, teres minor, subscapularis) stabilizing the shoulder.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Neurons & Neuroglia
summary_sections.content_html:
Neurons & Neuroglia

Neuron Structure

  • Cell body (soma): Contains the nucleus; integrates incoming signals.
  • Dendrites: Short, branching extensions that receive signals.
  • Axon: Single long process that conducts impulses away from the cell body.
  • Myelin sheath: Insulating lipid layer (Schwann cells in PNS, oligodendrocytes in CNS); speeds conduction.
  • Nodes of Ranvier: Gaps in myelin where saltatory (jumping) conduction occurs.

Neuroglia (Glial Cells)

  • Astrocytes: Support neurons, blood-brain barrier. Oligodendrocytes: CNS myelin. Schwann cells: PNS myelin.
  • Microglia: Immune defense in CNS. Ependymal cells: Line ventricles, produce CSF.

flashcards.definition: Functional unit of the nervous system; transmits electrical signals.
Branching extension of a neuron that receives incoming signals.
Long projection that carries nerve impulses away from the cell body.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Action Potentials & Synaptic Transmission
summary_sections.content_html:
Action Potentials & Synaptic Transmission

Resting Membrane Potential

At rest: −70 mV (inside negative). Maintained by Na⁺/K⁺ ATPase pump (3 Na⁺ out, 2 K⁺ in).

Action Potential Phases

  • Depolarization: Na⁺ channels open → Na⁺ rushes in → membrane becomes positive (~+30 mV).
  • Repolarization: Na⁺ channels close, K⁺ channels open → K⁺ exits → membrane returns to negative.
  • Hyperpolarization: Brief overshoot below −70 mV before return to rest.

Synaptic Transmission

AP reaches axon terminal → Ca²⁺ enters → vesicles release neurotransmitter → binds postsynaptic receptors → excitatory or inhibitory response.

flashcards.definition: ~−70 mV; maintained by Na⁺/K⁺ pump and ion channel leaks.
Na⁺ channels open; Na⁺ influx makes the interior positive.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Central Nervous System (Brain & Spinal Cord)
summary_sections.content_html:
Central Nervous System

Brain Regions

  • Cerebrum: Largest part; cerebral cortex handles higher functions (thought, memory, language, motor control). Four lobes: frontal, parietal, temporal, occipital.
  • Cerebellum: Coordination, balance, fine motor skills.
  • Brainstem: Midbrain, pons, medulla oblongata — vital functions (breathing, heart rate, sleep/wake).
  • Diencephalon: Thalamus (relay center), hypothalamus (homeostasis, hormones).

Spinal Cord

31 pairs of spinal nerves. Conducts signals between brain and body; integrates reflexes.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Peripheral Nervous System
summary_sections.content_html:
Peripheral Nervous System (PNS)

All neural tissue outside the brain and spinal cord.

Divisions

  • Sensory (afferent): Carries information from receptors TO the CNS.
  • Motor (efferent): Carries commands FROM the CNS to effectors.

Motor Subdivision

  • Somatic nervous system: Voluntary control of skeletal muscles.
  • Autonomic nervous system: Involuntary control of smooth muscle, cardiac muscle, glands.

Cranial Nerves

12 pairs of cranial nerves (I–XII) emerge from the brain. Important examples: vagus nerve (X) — extensive autonomic functions.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Autonomic Nervous System
summary_sections.content_html:
Autonomic Nervous System (ANS)

Involuntary control of internal organs, smooth muscle, cardiac muscle, and glands.

Two Divisions

  • Sympathetic ('fight or flight'): Increases heart rate, dilates bronchioles, redirects blood to muscles, dilates pupils, inhibits digestion.
  • Parasympathetic ('rest and digest'): Decreases heart rate, constricts bronchioles, stimulates digestion, constricts pupils.

Dual Innervation

Most organs receive both sympathetic and parasympathetic input, maintaining a dynamic balance.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Reflex Arcs
summary_sections.content_html:
Reflex Arcs

A reflex is a rapid, involuntary, predictable response to a stimulus.

Components of a Reflex Arc (5 steps)

  • 1. Receptor: Detects the stimulus.
  • 2. Sensory (afferent) neuron: Transmits impulse to the CNS.
  • 3. Integration center: CNS processes the information (may involve interneurons).
  • 4. Motor (efferent) neuron: Carries the response command.
  • 5. Effector: Muscle or gland that carries out the response.

Examples

Patellar (knee-jerk) reflex: Stretch receptor → sensory neuron → spinal cord → motor neuron → quadriceps contracts. This is a monosynaptic reflex (no interneuron).

flashcards.definition: Neural pathway for a reflex: receptor → sensory neuron → CNS → motor neuron → effector.
Only one synapse (sensory → motor); e.g., patellar reflex.
Multiple synapses involving interneurons; e.g., withdrawal reflex.
Knee-jerk reflex: tapping the patellar tendon stretches the quadriceps → leg kicks.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Disorders of the Nervous System
summary_sections.content_html:
Disorders of the Nervous System

  • Alzheimer's disease: Progressive memory loss and cognitive decline; amyloid plaques and neurofibrillary tangles.
  • Parkinson's disease: Loss of dopamine-producing neurons in substantia nigra → tremor, rigidity, bradykinesia.
  • Epilepsy: Abnormal, excessive electrical activity → seizures.
  • Multiple sclerosis: Autoimmune demyelination in the CNS.
  • Stroke: Ischemic (blockage) or hemorrhagic (bleed) → sudden neurological deficits.
  • Meningitis: Infection/inflammation of the meninges (bacterial or viral).

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Hormones & Chemical Signaling
summary_sections.content_html:
Hormones & Chemical Signaling

What Are Hormones?

Chemical messengers secreted by endocrine glands into the bloodstream; they act on distant target cells.

Types of Hormones

  • Amino acid-derived: Thyroid hormones, catecholamines (epinephrine, norepinephrine).
  • Peptide/protein: Insulin, growth hormone, ADH, oxytocin.
  • Steroid: Cortisol, estrogen, testosterone, aldosterone (derived from cholesterol).

Mechanisms of Action

  • Water-soluble hormones → bind surface receptors → second messenger (e.g., cAMP).
  • Lipid-soluble (steroid/thyroid) → pass through membrane → bind intracellular/nuclear receptors → alter gene expression.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Major Endocrine Glands
summary_sections.content_html:
Major Endocrine Glands

  • Pituitary (hypophysis): 'Master gland'; anterior (GH, TSH, ACTH, FSH, LH, prolactin), posterior (ADH, oxytocin — made in hypothalamus).
  • Thyroid: T3, T4 (metabolism); calcitonin (lowers blood Ca²⁺).
  • Parathyroids (4): PTH — raises blood Ca²⁺.
  • Adrenals: Cortex (cortisol, aldosterone, androgens); Medulla (epinephrine, norepinephrine).
  • Pancreas (endocrine): Islets of Langerhans — β-cells (insulin↓glucose), α-cells (glucagon↑glucose).
  • Gonads: Ovaries (estrogen, progesterone); Testes (testosterone).
  • Pineal gland: Melatonin (circadian rhythm).
  • Thymus: Thymosin (T-cell maturation).

flashcards.definition: 'Master gland'; anterior lobe produces GH, TSH, ACTH, FSH, LH, prolactin; posterior stores ADH, oxytocin.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Hormonal Regulation of Homeostasis
summary_sections.content_html:
Hormonal Regulation of Homeostasis

Blood Glucose Regulation

High glucose → β-cells release insulin → cells absorb glucose → blood glucose drops. Low glucose → α-cells release glucagon → liver releases glucose → blood glucose rises.

Calcium Homeostasis

High Ca²⁺ → calcitonin → Ca²⁺ into bone. Low Ca²⁺ → PTH → Ca²⁺ out of bone + ↑intestinal absorption + ↑renal reabsorption.

Water Balance

Dehydration → hypothalamus detects ↑osmolarity → ADH released → kidneys reabsorb more water.

flashcards.definition: Insulin (↓glucose) and glucagon (↑glucose) maintain blood sugar within a narrow range.
PTH raises Ca²⁺; calcitonin lowers Ca²⁺; vitamin D aids absorption.
Released when dehydrated; causes kidneys to reabsorb more water, concentrating urine.
Response reduces the original stimulus; primary mechanism for hormonal regulation.
Maintenance of stable internal conditions despite external changes.

u5_l5.4.title: Feedback Loops in the Endocrine System

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Feedback Loops in the Endocrine System
summary_sections.content_html:
Feedback Loops in the Endocrine System

Negative Feedback (Most Common)

Output reduces the stimulus. Example: ↑cortisol → inhibits hypothalamus (CRH) and anterior pituitary (ACTH) → less cortisol produced.

Positive Feedback (Rare)

Output amplifies the stimulus. Examples: oxytocin during labor (uterine contractions ↑ → more oxytocin → stronger contractions until delivery) and LH surge triggering ovulation.

Hypothalamic-Pituitary Axes

HPT axis (thyroid), HPA axis (adrenal), HPG axis (gonadal) — all use negative feedback to maintain set points.

flashcards.definition: The dominant control mechanism; the product inhibits further production to maintain a set point.
Rare; the product amplifies the stimulus (e.g., oxytocin in labor, LH surge).
Hypothalamic-Pituitary-Adrenal axis: CRH → ACTH → cortisol; cortisol inhibits CRH and ACTH.
Hypothalamic-Pituitary-Thyroid axis: TRH → TSH → T3/T4; T3/T4 inhibit TRH and TSH.
Hypothalamic-Pituitary-Gonadal axis: GnRH → FSH/LH → sex hormones; sex hormones feed back.

quiz_questions.explanation: Product shuts off its own production.

options.text: Inhibits TRH and TSH (reducing thyroid stimulation)
Inhibits CRH and ACTH (reducing cortisol production)

quiz_questions.question_text: An example of positive feedback is:

options.text: Oxytocin during labor (contractions → more oxytocin → stronger contractions)

quiz_questions.explanation: Amplification until the event is complete.

quiz_questions.question_text: The LH surge that triggers ovulation is an example of:

options.text: Positive feedback (rising estrogen triggers an LH spike)

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Disorders of the Endocrine System
summary_sections.content_html:
Disorders of the Endocrine System

  • Diabetes mellitus: Type 1 (autoimmune β-cell destruction) and Type 2 (insulin resistance). Hyperglycemia → complications (neuropathy, retinopathy, nephropathy).
  • Hyperthyroidism: Graves' disease (autoimmune → excess T3/T4). Symptoms: weight loss, tachycardia, exophthalmos.
  • Hypothyroidism: Hashimoto's (autoimmune thyroid destruction). Symptoms: fatigue, weight gain, cold intolerance.
  • Cushing's syndrome: Excess cortisol. Addison's disease: Adrenal insufficiency.
  • Gigantism/Acromegaly: Excess GH before/after bone growth plate closure.

flashcards.definition: Chronic hyperglycemia; Type 1 (no insulin) vs. Type 2 (insulin resistance).

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Case Studies in Endocrinology
summary_sections.content_html:
Case Studies in Endocrinology

Case 1: A Patient with Uncontrolled Blood Sugar

A 45-year-old presents with polyuria, polydipsia, and weight loss despite increased appetite. Fasting glucose 280 mg/dL, HbA1c 10.5%. Diagnosis: Type 2 diabetes mellitus. Management: lifestyle changes + metformin + monitoring.

Case 2: Thyroid Dysfunction

A 30-year-old female with weight gain, fatigue, constipation, cold intolerance. TSH elevated, T4 low. Anti-TPO antibodies positive. Diagnosis: Hashimoto's thyroiditis. Treatment: levothyroxine.

Case 3: Adrenal Crisis

A patient on chronic prednisone suddenly stops medication → collapse, hypotension, hypoglycemia. Adrenal crisis due to HPA axis suppression. Treatment: IV hydrocortisone, fluid resuscitation.

flashcards.definition: The 'three Ps' of diabetes: excessive urination, thirst, and hunger.
Glycated hemoglobin; reflects average blood glucose over ~3 months.
Antibodies against thyroid peroxidase; marker for Hashimoto's thyroiditis.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: AP Prep: Hormone Pathways
summary_sections.content_html:
AP Prep: Hormone Pathways

Essential Pathways for the Exam

  • HPT axis: Hypothalamus (TRH) → Anterior pituitary (TSH) → Thyroid (T3/T4) → Negative feedback to hypothalamus/pituitary.
  • HPA axis: Hypothalamus (CRH) → Anterior pituitary (ACTH) → Adrenal cortex (cortisol) → Negative feedback.
  • HPG axis: Hypothalamus (GnRH) → Anterior pituitary (FSH/LH) → Gonads (estrogen/testosterone) → Negative feedback.
  • Blood glucose: Insulin/glucagon antagonism.
  • Calcium: PTH/calcitonin/vitamin D interplay.

CRH → ACTH → cortisol; stress axis; negative feedback from cortisol.
GnRH → FSH/LH → sex hormones; reproductive regulation.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Structure of the Heart
summary_sections.content_html:
Structure of the Heart

Chambers & Valves

  • 4 chambers: Right atrium (RA), right ventricle (RV), left atrium (LA), left ventricle (LV).
  • AV valves: Tricuspid (right) and mitral/bicuspid (left) — prevent backflow from ventricles to atria.
  • Semilunar valves: Pulmonary (RV → pulmonary artery) and aortic (LV → aorta).

Layers & Coverings

  • Pericardium: Protective sac. Epicardium: Outer. Myocardium: Thick muscular middle. Endocardium: Inner lining.

Blood Flow Path

Body → vena cavae → RA → tricuspid → RV → pulmonary valve → lungs → pulmonary veins → LA → mitral → LV → aortic valve → aorta → body.

flashcards.definition: Pumps deoxygenated blood to the lungs via the pulmonary artery.
Pumps oxygenated blood to the body via the aorta; has the thickest wall.
AV valve between the right atrium and right ventricle (3 cusps).
AV valve between the left atrium and left ventricle (2 cusps).
The thick muscular layer of the heart wall; responsible for contraction.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Blood Vessels & Circulation
summary_sections.content_html:
Blood Vessels & Circulation

Vessel Types

  • Arteries: Thick walls, carry blood AWAY from the heart. Elastic (aorta) and muscular (distributing).
  • Arterioles: Small arteries that regulate blood flow to capillary beds.
  • Capillaries: Thin-walled (one cell thick), site of gas/nutrient/waste exchange.
  • Venules → Veins: Return blood to the heart. Thinner walls; valves prevent backflow.

Circulatory Routes

Pulmonary: RV → lungs → LA. Systemic: LV → body → RA. Hepatic portal: GI tract → liver → hepatic veins → IVC.

flashcards.definition: Thick-walled vessel carrying blood away from the heart; high pressure.
Thinnest vessel (one endothelial cell thick); site of exchange between blood and tissues.
Thin-walled vessel carrying blood toward the heart; has valves to prevent backflow.
RV → pulmonary artery → lungs (gas exchange) → pulmonary veins → LA.
Venous blood from GI tract goes to the liver for processing before returning to the heart.

options.text: Gas, nutrient, and waste exchange between blood and tissues

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Blood Composition & Function
summary_sections.content_html:
Blood Composition & Function

Plasma (~55%)

Water (~92%), proteins (albumin, globulins, fibrinogen), electrolytes, nutrients, waste, gases, hormones.

Formed Elements (~45%)

  • Red blood cells (RBCs/erythrocytes): Carry O₂ (hemoglobin); no nucleus; ~4.5–5.5 million/µL.
  • White blood cells (WBCs/leukocytes): Immune defense; 5,000–10,000/µL. Types: neutrophils, lymphocytes, monocytes, eosinophils, basophils.
  • Platelets (thrombocytes): Cell fragments; essential for clotting; 150,000–400,000/µL.

flashcards.definition: Liquid component of blood (~55%); water, proteins, electrolytes, nutrients, waste.
Red blood cell; contains hemoglobin for O₂ transport; no nucleus.
White blood cell; immune defense. Types: neutrophils, lymphocytes, monocytes, eosinophils, basophils.
Cell fragment from megakaryocytes; essential for blood clotting (hemostasis).

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Cardiac Cycle & Heartbeat Regulation
summary_sections.content_html:
Cardiac Cycle & Heartbeat Regulation

Cardiac Cycle

Systole: Contraction phase (ventricles pump blood). Diastole: Relaxation phase (ventricles fill).

Conduction System

  • SA node (sinoatrial): 'Pacemaker'; initiates each heartbeat (~60-100 bpm).
  • AV node: Delays signal briefly → ensures atria finish emptying.
  • Bundle of His → bundle branches → Purkinje fibers: Conduct impulse rapidly through ventricles.

ECG/EKG

P wave = atrial depolarization. QRS complex = ventricular depolarization. T wave = ventricular repolarization.

flashcards.definition: Contraction phase; ventricles eject blood into arteries.
Relaxation phase; ventricles fill with blood from atria.
Sinoatrial node; the heart's natural pacemaker (sets the rhythm).
Atrioventricular node; delays the impulse so atria contract before ventricles.
Electrocardiogram; records electrical activity: P wave, QRS complex, T wave.

quiz_questions.question_text: The SA node is called the pacemaker because it:

quiz_questions.question_text: The AV node's main role is to:

options.text: Delay the impulse briefly so atria empty before ventricles contract

quiz_questions.explanation: Brief delay = atria finish before ventricles start.

quiz_questions.question_text: The Bundle of His conducts impulses from:

options.text: AV node to the ventricular septum (then to bundle branches)

quiz_questions.explanation: Pathway from AV node to ventricles.

options.text: The ventricular walls (causing synchronized ventricular contraction)

quiz_questions.explanation: CO = amount of blood pumped per minute.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Blood Pressure & Homeostasis
summary_sections.content_html:
Blood Pressure & Homeostasis

Blood Pressure Basics

Systolic pressure: Pressure during ventricular contraction (~120 mmHg). Diastolic: Pressure during relaxation (~80 mmHg). Normal = 120/80.

Regulation

  • Short-term (neural): Baroreceptors in carotid sinus/aortic arch → ANS adjusts HR and vessel diameter.
  • Long-term (renal/hormonal): RAAS system, ADH, ANP — regulate blood volume.

Hypertension

Persistently elevated BP (≥130/80). Major risk for heart attack, stroke, kidney disease.

flashcards.definition: Peak pressure during ventricular contraction (top number); normal ~120 mmHg.
Lowest pressure during ventricular relaxation (bottom number); normal ~80 mmHg.
Pressure sensors in carotid sinus and aortic arch; detect BP changes for short-term regulation.
Renin-Angiotensin-Aldosterone System; long-term BP regulation via blood volume control.
Chronically elevated blood pressure (≥130/80); 'silent killer'; major cardiovascular risk factor.

options.text: Changes in blood pressure (stretch of vessel walls)

The RAAS system is activated by:

options.text: Potent vasoconstriction and stimulates aldosterone release → ↑BP
Causing kidneys to retain sodium and water → ↑blood volume

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Disorders of the Circulatory System
summary_sections.content_html:
Disorders of the Circulatory System

  • Coronary artery disease (CAD): Atherosclerosis of coronary arteries → angina, myocardial infarction (heart attack).
  • Heart failure: Heart can't pump effectively; systolic (reduced ejection fraction) or diastolic (preserved EF).
  • Valvular disease: Stenosis (narrowing) or regurgitation (leaking).
  • Peripheral artery disease (PAD): Atherosclerosis in limb arteries → claudication.
  • Aneurysm: Weakened arterial wall bulges → risk of rupture.

flashcards.definition: Atherosclerosis of coronary arteries; can cause angina or heart attack (MI).

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Case Studies in Cardiovascular Health
summary_sections.content_html:
Case Studies in Cardiovascular Health

Case 1: Acute MI

A 62-year-old male with chest pain radiating to left arm, diaphoresis, ECG showing ST elevation in leads II, III, aVF. Troponin elevated. Diagnosis: inferior STEMI. Treatment: emergent PCI (percutaneous coronary intervention).

Case 2: Congestive Heart Failure

A 70-year-old with progressive dyspnea, pedal edema, and pulmonary crackles. Echocardiogram shows ejection fraction 25%. Diagnosis: systolic heart failure. Management: ACE inhibitor, beta-blocker, diuretic.

Case 3: Hypertensive Emergency

BP 220/130 with headache and blurred vision. Requires IV antihypertensives with careful BP reduction.

flashcards.definition: ST-Elevation Myocardial Infarction; complete coronary artery blockage requiring emergent reperfusion.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: AP Prep: ECG Interpretation
summary_sections.content_html:
AP Prep: ECG Interpretation

ECG Waveforms Review

  • P wave: Atrial depolarization (atrial contraction).
  • PR interval: Time from atrial depolarization to ventricular depolarization (0.12–0.20 s).
  • QRS complex: Ventricular depolarization (0.06–0.10 s).
  • T wave: Ventricular repolarization.
  • QT interval: Total ventricular electrical activity.

Common Findings

Normal sinus rhythm, sinus bradycardia, sinus tachycardia, atrial fibrillation (no P waves, irregular), ST elevation (MI), heart block.

flashcards.definition: Atrial depolarization; should precede every QRS in normal sinus rhythm.
Time from start of P to start of QRS (0.12-0.20 s); prolonged in heart block.
Ventricular depolarization; normally 0.06-0.10 s wide.
Between QRS and T wave; elevation or depression indicates ischemia/injury.
Irregular rhythm with no identifiable P waves; atria quiver instead of contracting.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Anatomy of the Respiratory System
summary_sections.content_html:
Anatomy of the Respiratory System

Upper Respiratory Tract

  • Nose/nasal cavity: Warms, moistens, filters air. Pharynx: Throat; shared with digestive tract.
  • Larynx: Voice box; epiglottis prevents food from entering the trachea.

Lower Respiratory Tract

  • Trachea: Windpipe; C-shaped cartilage rings.
  • Bronchi → bronchioles → terminal bronchioles → respiratory bronchioles → alveolar ducts → alveoli.
  • Alveoli: ~300 million; thin-walled sacs where gas exchange occurs.
  • Lungs: Right (3 lobes), left (2 lobes). Pleura: Serous membrane lining.

flashcards.definition: Tiny air sacs (~300 million) in the lungs where O₂/CO₂ exchange occurs with capillaries.
Windpipe; supported by C-shaped cartilage rings; leads to the bronchi.
Voice box; contains vocal cords; epiglottis guards the airway during swallowing.
Two main branches of the trachea (right and left); carry air into each lung.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Mechanics of Breathing
summary_sections.content_html:
Mechanics of Breathing

Inspiration (Inhalation)

Diaphragm contracts (flattens) + external intercostals lift ribs → thoracic volume increases → intrapulmonary pressure drops below atmospheric → air flows in.

Expiration (Exhalation)

Passive at rest: diaphragm relaxes (domes up) + elastic recoil of lungs → thoracic volume decreases → pressure increases → air flows out. Forced expiration uses internal intercostals and abdominal muscles.

Lung Volumes

Tidal volume (~500 mL), vital capacity, residual volume, total lung capacity. Spirometry measures these.

flashcards.definition: Active process; diaphragm contracts, thoracic volume increases, pressure drops, air flows in.
Passive at rest (elastic recoil); forced expiration uses internal intercostals and abdominals.
Volume of air inhaled/exhaled in a normal breath (~500 mL).
Maximum air exhaled after maximum inhalation (~4.8 L).
Air remaining in lungs after maximum exhalation (~1.2 L); prevents alveolar collapse.

Drops below atmospheric pressure → air flows in

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Gas Exchange in the Alveoli
summary_sections.content_html:
Gas Exchange in the Alveoli

Principles

Gas exchange occurs by simple diffusion across the respiratory membrane. O₂ diffuses from alveoli (high PO₂ ~104 mmHg) into pulmonary capillary blood (low PO₂ ~40 mmHg). CO₂ diffuses from blood (PCO₂ ~45 mmHg) into alveoli (PCO₂ ~40 mmHg).

Factors Affecting Exchange

  • Surface area: ~70 m²; reduced in emphysema.
  • Membrane thickness: Thickened in fibrosis → impaired diffusion.
  • Ventilation-perfusion (V/Q) matching: Adequate airflow matched with blood flow for efficient exchange.

flashcards.definition: The pressure exerted by a single gas in a mixture; drives diffusion.
Alveolar epithelium + capillary endothelium; ~0.5 µm; where gas exchange occurs.
Ideal ratio of alveolar airflow to capillary blood flow for efficient gas exchange.
Destructive lung disease that reduces alveolar surface area → impaired gas exchange.
Thickening/scarring of lung tissue increasing diffusion distance → impaired gas exchange.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Transport of O₂ & CO₂
summary_sections.content_html:
Transport of O₂ & CO₂

Oxygen Transport

  • ~98.5% bound to hemoglobin (Hb) → oxyhemoglobin (HbO₂).
  • ~1.5% dissolved in plasma.
  • O₂-Hb dissociation curve: Sigmoidal; right shift (↑temp, ↓pH, ↑CO₂, ↑2,3-BPG) → easier O₂ release to tissues.

CO₂ Transport

  • ~70% as bicarbonate (HCO₃⁻) via carbonic anhydrase: CO₂ + H₂O → H₂CO₃ → H⁺ + HCO₃⁻.
  • ~23% bound to Hb (carbaminohemoglobin). ~7% dissolved.

flashcards.definition: Hemoglobin bound to O₂ (HbO₂); forms in the lungs, releases O₂ in tissues.
Sigmoidal curve showing Hb's O₂ saturation vs. PO₂; right shift = easier O₂ unloading.
Primary form of CO₂ transport in blood (~70%); formed by carbonic anhydrase in RBCs.
Enzyme in RBCs catalyzing CO₂ + H₂O ↔ H₂CO₃ ↔ H⁺ + HCO₃⁻.
Increased H⁺ or CO₂ decreases Hb's affinity for O₂ → promotes O₂ release in active tissues.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Regulation of Breathing
summary_sections.content_html:
Regulation of Breathing

Neural Control

  • Medullary respiratory center: Sets the basic rhythm — dorsal respiratory group (inspiration), ventral respiratory group (forced breathing).
  • Pontine respiratory group: Smooths transitions between inspiration and expiration.

Chemical Control

  • Central chemoreceptors (medulla): Detect ↑CO₂ (via ↑H⁺ in CSF) — primary driver of ventilation.
  • Peripheral chemoreceptors (carotid/aortic bodies): Detect ↓O₂, ↑CO₂, ↓pH.

flashcards.definition: Brainstem region that generates the basic rhythm of breathing.
In the medulla; primarily sense ↑CO₂ (via H⁺ in CSF) to increase ventilation.
In carotid/aortic bodies; detect low O₂, high CO₂, low pH.
Stretch receptors in lungs prevent overinflation by inhibiting inspiration.
Normal stimulus to breathe: rising CO₂ detected by chemoreceptors → increased ventilation.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Disorders of the Respiratory System
summary_sections.content_html:
Disorders of the Respiratory System

  • Asthma: Chronic airway inflammation with reversible bronchospasm → wheezing, dyspnea. Treated with bronchodilators and inhaled corticosteroids.
  • COPD: Chronic bronchitis (excess mucus) + emphysema (alveolar destruction). Mostly from smoking. Irreversible airflow limitation.
  • Pneumonia: Infection (bacterial, viral, fungal) filling alveoli with fluid/pus → impaired gas exchange.
  • Tuberculosis (TB): Mycobacterium tuberculosis; granuloma formation.
  • Lung cancer: Leading cancer killer; strong association with smoking.

flashcards.definition: Chronic inflammatory airway disease with reversible bronchospasm; wheezing, dyspnea.
Chronic Obstructive Pulmonary Disease; chronic bronchitis + emphysema; usually from smoking.
Lung infection causing alveolar fluid/pus accumulation → impaired gas exchange.
Destruction of alveolar walls reducing surface area; irreversible; component of COPD.
Blood clot (usually from DVT) blocking a pulmonary artery; life-threatening.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Case Studies in Pulmonology
summary_sections.content_html:
Case Studies in Pulmonology

Case 1: Acute Asthma Exacerbation

A 22-year-old with wheezing, dyspnea, and prolonged expiratory phase after allergen exposure. Peak flow 50% of predicted. Treatment: nebulized albuterol + systemic corticosteroids. Improvement within hours.

Case 2: Community-Acquired Pneumonia

A 68-year-old with fever, productive cough (rusty sputum), dyspnea. CXR: right lower lobe consolidation. WBC elevated. Treatment: antibiotics (empiric then targeted).

Case 3: COPD Exacerbation

A 65-year-old smoker with worsening dyspnea, increased sputum, and hypoxemia. ABG: PaO₂ 55, PaCO₂ 60 (respiratory acidosis). Treatment: controlled O₂, bronchodilators, antibiotics, systemic steroids.

flashcards.definition: Measures maximum airflow during forced exhalation; reduced in asthma/COPD.
Solid-appearing area on CXR where alveoli are filled with fluid/pus (e.g., pneumonia).
Blood-tinged sputum characteristic of Streptococcus pneumoniae pneumonia.
In COPD, O₂ is given at low flow to avoid suppressing the hypoxic drive.
pH < 7.35 with PaCO₂ > 45 mmHg; caused by hypoventilation/CO₂ retention.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: AP Prep: Respiratory Physiology
summary_sections.content_html:
AP Prep: Respiratory Physiology

Key Topics for Exam

  • Breathing mechanics: Boyle's law, diaphragm, intrapulmonary vs. intrapleural pressure.
  • Gas laws: Dalton's (partial pressures), Henry's (gas solubility).
  • O₂ transport: Hb saturation curve, Bohr effect, factors shifting curve.
  • CO₂ transport: bicarbonate, carbaminohemoglobin, dissolved.
  • Regulation: chemoreceptors (central vs. peripheral), respiratory centers.
  • Acid-base: respiratory acidosis/alkalosis, compensation.

flashcards.definition: P₁V₁ = P₂V₂; as volume increases, pressure decreases (and vice versa).
Total pressure of a gas mixture = sum of each gas's partial pressure.
Amount of gas dissolved in liquid is proportional to its partial pressure.
pH < 7.35, PaCO₂ > 45 mmHg; caused by hypoventilation.
pH > 7.45, PaCO₂ < 35 mmHg; caused by hyperventilation.

options.text: As thoracic volume increases (inspiration), pressure decreases, drawing air in

quiz_questions.question_text: If intrapleural pressure becomes equal to atmospheric (e.g., chest wound):

options.text: Individual gas partial pressures drive diffusion (each gas moves independently)

options.text: More gas dissolves in blood at higher partial pressures

quiz_questions.explanation: Nearly fully saturated in the lungs.

quiz_questions.question_text: At a PO₂ of ~40 mmHg (venous blood), Hb saturation is approximately:
quiz_questions.explanation: Hb releases ~25% of its O₂ to tissues.

quiz_questions.question_text: The P50 is the PO₂ at which Hb is _____ saturated.
A right shift (increased P50) means the tissue gets:

options.text: More O₂ (Hb releases O₂ more readily)

quiz_questions.explanation: Active tissues benefit from right shift.

quiz_questions.question_text: Respiratory acidosis is compensated by the kidneys, which:

options.text: Retain HCO₃⁻ and excrete H⁺ (renal compensation takes hours-days)

quiz_questions.explanation: Kidneys balance pH by adjusting bicarbonate.

quiz_questions.explanation: Kidneys lower bicarb to lower pH.

quiz_questions.explanation: High pH + low CO₂ + normal bicarb.

options.text: Alveolar and arterial PO₂ → improved oxygenation

options.text: Increasing ventilation → blowing off excess CO₂

options.text: Directly linked (ventilation adjusts CO₂ → adjusts pH)

quiz_questions.explanation: Breathing is a rapid pH regulator.

options.text: Cardiovascular, renal, and metabolic systems for whole-body homeostasis

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Anatomy of Digestive Organs
summary_sections.content_html:
Anatomy of Digestive Organs

GI Tract (Alimentary Canal)

  • Mouth → pharynx → esophagus → stomach → small intestine (duodenum, jejunum, ileum) → large intestine (cecum, colon, rectum) → anus.

GI Wall Layers

  • Mucosa: Innermost; epithelium, secretion/absorption. Submucosa: Connective tissue, blood vessels, nerves.
  • Muscularis: Smooth muscle (circular + longitudinal) for peristalsis. Serosa: Outermost covering (visceral peritoneum).

Accessory Organs

Salivary glands, liver, gallbladder, pancreas — secrete into the GI tract via ducts.

flashcards.definition: The continuous tube from mouth to anus through which food passes.
Wave-like smooth muscle contractions that propel food through the GI tract.
Innermost GI wall layer; responsible for secretion and absorption.
Primary site of digestion and absorption; three parts: duodenum, jejunum, ileum.
Serous membrane lining the abdominal cavity and covering most abdominal organs.

quiz_questions.question_text: The correct order of the GI tract is:

options.text: Mouth → pharynx → esophagus → stomach → small intestine → large intestine → anus

Salivary glands, liver, gallbladder, and pancreas

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Mechanical & Chemical Digestion
summary_sections.content_html:
Mechanical & Chemical Digestion

Mechanical Digestion

Mastication (chewing), churning in the stomach, segmentation in the small intestine. Increases surface area for enzymes.

Chemical Digestion

  • Carbohydrates: Salivary amylase (mouth) → pancreatic amylase (SI) → maltase, sucrase, lactase (brush border) → monosaccharides.
  • Proteins: Pepsin (stomach, pH 2) → pancreatic trypsin, chymotrypsin (SI) → peptidases (brush border) → amino acids.
  • Fats: Bile salts emulsify → pancreatic lipase → monoglycerides + fatty acids.

flashcards.definition: Physical breakdown (chewing, churning, segmentation) increasing surface area for enzymes.
Enzymatic breakdown of macromolecules into absorbable units (monosaccharides, amino acids, fatty acids).
Enzyme in saliva that begins starch digestion in the mouth.
Protein-digesting enzyme active in the stomach's acidic environment (pH ~2).
Produced by liver, stored in gallbladder; emulsifies fats for lipase action.

quiz_questions.question_text: Mechanical digestion in the mouth is called:

options.text: Activating pepsin, killing microbes, and denaturing proteins

quiz_questions.explanation: Liver makes bile; gallbladder stores/concentrates it.

options.text: Breaking large fat globules into small droplets (increases surface area for lipase)

options.text: Small intestine (on the microvilli of enterocytes)
Deficiency of the enzyme lactase (can't digest dairy sugar)
Mixes chyme with digestive juices (back-and-forth contractions)
Semi-liquid mixture of food and gastric juices in the stomach

options.text: Acid in the duodenum → stimulates pancreatic bicarbonate secretion

options.text: Stimulate HCl secretion and gastric motility

quiz_questions.explanation: Positive feedback for acid secretion during a meal.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Absorption of Nutrients
summary_sections.content_html:
Absorption of Nutrients

Small Intestine = Primary Absorption Site

  • Villi and microvilli provide ~200 m² surface area.
  • Monosaccharides & amino acids: Absorbed into capillaries → portal vein → liver.
  • Fatty acids & monoglycerides: Absorbed into enterocytes → re-esterified to triglycerides → packaged into chylomicrons → enter lacteals (lymphatic) → eventually blood.
  • Vitamins: Fat-soluble (A, D, E, K) absorbed with fats; water-soluble absorbed with water.

Large Intestine

Absorbs remaining water and electrolytes; houses gut microbiome; forms and stores feces.

flashcards.definition: Finger-like projections in the small intestine; each contains capillaries and a lacteal for absorption.
Microscopic projections on enterocytes; vastly increase surface area for absorption.
Lipoprotein particle that transports dietary fats from enterocytes into lymphatic lacteals.
Lymphatic capillary within each villus; absorbs dietary fats (chylomicrons).
Carries nutrient-rich blood from the GI tract to the liver for processing.

quiz_questions.question_text: The primary site of nutrient absorption is the:
Villi and microvilli increase the small intestine's surface area to approximately:

options.text: Blood capillaries in the villi → hepatic portal vein → liver
Blood capillaries → hepatic portal vein → liver

quiz_questions.explanation: Amino acids enter the portal circulation.

options.text: Enterocytes → packaged as chylomicrons → enter lacteals (lymphatic system)
Lipoprotein particles that carry dietary fats through the lymph
Lymphatic capillaries within each villus (absorb fats)

quiz_questions.question_text: Fat-soluble vitamins (A, D, E, K) are absorbed:

options.text: With dietary fats (require bile for absorption)

quiz_questions.explanation: Impaired fat absorption → fat-soluble vitamin deficiency.

options.text: Intrinsic factor (produced by parietal cells) — absorbed in the ileum

quiz_questions.explanation: IF binds B₁₂ for ileal absorption.

quiz_questions.question_text: The hepatic portal system carries absorbed nutrients to the:

options.text: Liver for processing, detoxification, and metabolism

quiz_questions.question_text: The gut microbiome in the large intestine:

options.text: Synthesizes vitamins (K, biotin), aids digestion, and supports immunity
Gluten triggers immune-mediated destruction of small intestinal villi

quiz_questions.question_text: Iron is primarily absorbed in the:

options.text: Weight loss, nutrient deficiencies, diarrhea, and anemia

quiz_questions.explanation: Among the fastest-dividing cells in the body.

options.text: Sodium + glucose co-transport drives water absorption — critical for treating dehydration
Managing malnutrition, celiac disease, Crohn's disease, short bowel syndrome, and more

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Accessory Organs of Digestion
summary_sections.content_html:
Accessory Organs of Digestion

Liver

Largest internal organ. Functions: bile production, detoxification, protein synthesis (albumin, clotting factors), glycogen storage, bilirubin metabolism.

Gallbladder

Stores and concentrates bile; releases it into the duodenum upon CCK stimulation.

Pancreas (Exocrine)

Produces pancreatic juice: bicarbonate (neutralizes acid), amylase, lipase, trypsinogen, chymotrypsinogen, nucleases.

Salivary Glands

Parotid, submandibular, sublingual — produce saliva (water, amylase, lysozyme, mucin).

flashcards.definition: Largest internal organ; bile production, detoxification, protein synthesis, storage.
Stores and concentrates bile; releases into duodenum when stimulated by CCK.
Produces bicarbonate and digestive enzymes (amylase, lipase, proteases).
Breakdown product of hemoglobin; processed by the liver; excess → jaundice.
Produce saliva containing water, amylase (starch digestion), mucin, and lysozyme.

options.text: Albumin, clotting factors (fibrinogen, prothrombin), and others

options.text: Bilirubin accumulates in the blood (liver disease, bile duct obstruction, or hemolysis)

Neutralize acidic chyme entering the duodenum

options.text: Inflammation of the liver (viral: A, B, C; or alcoholic, autoimmune)
Chronic liver scarring (fibrosis) replacing functional tissue

options.text: Largest salivary gland (produces serous, enzyme-rich saliva)
Carries bile from the liver/gallbladder to the duodenum (joins pancreatic duct at ampulla of Vater)

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Disorders of the Digestive System
summary_sections.content_html:
Disorders of the Digestive System

  • GERD: Gastroesophageal reflux — acid damages esophagus. Treatment: PPIs, lifestyle changes.
  • Peptic ulcer: Mucosal erosion in stomach/duodenum; often H. pylori or NSAID-related.
  • IBD: Crohn's disease (any GI segment, transmural) vs. ulcerative colitis (colon only, mucosal).
  • IBS: Functional disorder — altered motility without structural cause.
  • Colorectal cancer: Third most common cancer; screening via colonoscopy.
  • Celiac disease: Autoimmune response to gluten → villous atrophy → malabsorption.

flashcards.definition: Gastroesophageal reflux disease; chronic acid reflux damaging the esophagus.
Erosion of the stomach or duodenal lining; H. pylori and NSAIDs are common causes.
IBD that can affect any GI segment (mouth to anus); transmural inflammation.
IBD limited to the colon; superficial (mucosal) inflammation with continuous lesions.
Autoimmune disorder triggered by gluten; destroys intestinal villi → malabsorption.

options.text: Chronic gastric acid reflux into the esophagus (LES dysfunction)

quiz_questions.explanation: Colonoscopy detects and removes precancerous polyps.

quiz_questions.explanation: Squamous → columnar metaplasia; increases cancer risk.

options.text: Right lower quadrant abdominal pain (McBurney's point), fever, nausea
Inflammation/infection of diverticula (outpouchings) in the colon wall

quiz_questions.explanation: Common in older adults; linked to low-fiber diet.

options.text: Swollen/inflamed veins in the rectal area

options.text: Dehydration from fluid loss is the most dangerous consequence

quiz_questions.explanation: Dietary fiber supports overall GI function.

u8_l8.6.title: Case Studies in Nutrition & GI Health

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Case Studies in Nutrition & GI Health
summary_sections.content_html:
Case Studies in Nutrition & GI Health

Case 1: Iron-Deficiency Anemia

A 28-year-old vegetarian female with fatigue, pallor, low Hb, low ferritin, microcytic hypochromic RBCs. Diagnosis: iron-deficiency anemia. Treatment: iron supplementation + vitamin C to enhance absorption + dietary counseling.

Case 2: Acute Pancreatitis

A 50-year-old male with epigastric pain radiating to back after heavy alcohol use. Elevated lipase. Diagnosis: acute pancreatitis. Management: NPO (nothing by mouth), IV fluids, pain control, alcohol cessation.

Case 3: Celiac Disease

A child with failure to thrive, chronic diarrhea, and abdominal distension. Anti-tTG antibodies positive; duodenal biopsy shows villous atrophy. Treatment: strict gluten-free diet.

flashcards.definition: Storage form of iron; low ferritin = iron stores depleted.
Small, pale RBCs characteristic of iron-deficiency anemia.
Elevated serum lipase is the most specific marker for acute pancreatitis.

quiz_questions.explanation: GET SMASHED mnemonic (Gallstones, Ethanol top the list).

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: AP Prep: Digestive Physiology

AP Prep: Digestive Physiology

Key Topics for Exam

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Anatomy of the Urinary System
summary_sections.content_html:
Anatomy of the Urinary System

Components

  • Kidneys (2): Bean-shaped, retroperitoneal. Cortex (outer), medulla (inner pyramids), pelvis (collects urine).
  • Ureters: Transport urine from kidneys to bladder via peristalsis.
  • Urinary Bladder: Stores urine; detrusor muscle; trigone (triangle between two ureteral openings and internal urethral orifice).
  • Urethra: Carries urine from bladder to exterior. Female ~4 cm; male ~20 cm (passes through prostate).

Nephron — Functional Unit

~1 million per kidney. Parts: glomerulus (Bowman's capsule), PCT, loop of Henle, DCT, collecting duct.

flashcards.definition: Functional unit of the kidney; filters blood and produces urine (~1 million per kidney).
Capillary tuft inside Bowman's capsule; site of blood filtration.
Outer region of the kidney containing glomeruli and convoluted tubules.
Inner region of the kidney containing loops of Henle and collecting ducts (pyramids).
Smooth muscle of the urinary bladder wall; contracts during urination.

quiz_questions.question_text: The functional unit of the kidney is the:
quiz_questions.explanation: Nephron = complete filtration and reabsorption unit.

options.text: Retroperitoneally (behind the peritoneum) in the posterior abdominal wall
Glomeruli, proximal and distal convoluted tubules
Loops of Henle, collecting ducts, and vasa recta (arranged in pyramids)
Collects urine from the collecting ducts and funnels it to the ureter

quiz_questions.question_text: The trigone of the bladder is:

options.text: A triangular area formed by the two ureteral openings and the internal urethral orifice

quiz_questions.explanation: Clinically significant; common site of infection.
Shorter → higher UTI risk in females.

quiz_questions.explanation: Prostatic enlargement can obstruct urine flow.

options.text: Concentration gradient in the medulla (countercurrent multiplier)
Is regulated by aldosterone (Na⁺ reabsorption) and PTH (Ca²⁺ reabsorption)
