options.text: UTIs, kidney stones, renal failure, and urinary obstruction

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Kidney Function & Filtration
summary_sections.content_html:
Kidney Function & Filtration

Three Processes of Urine Formation

  • Glomerular filtration: Passive, pressure-driven. GFR ~125 mL/min = ~180 L/day. Filters water, ions, glucose, amino acids, urea; retains proteins and cells.
  • Tubular reabsorption: PCT (65% of filtrate), loop of Henle (water & ions), DCT & collecting duct (hormone-regulated). >99% of filtrate reabsorbed.
  • Tubular secretion: H⁺, K⁺, drugs, toxins secreted from blood into tubular fluid.

RAAS

Low BP → renin → angiotensinogen → angiotensin I → (ACE) → angiotensin II → vasoconstriction + aldosterone → Na⁺/H₂O retention → BP ↑.

flashcards.definition: Volume of filtrate formed per minute (~125 mL/min); key measure of kidney function.
Process of moving useful substances (glucose, water, ions) from tubular filtrate back to blood.
Process of moving substances (H⁺, K⁺, drugs) from blood into tubular fluid for excretion.
Renin-Angiotensin-Aldosterone System; regulates blood pressure and fluid balance.
Released from posterior pituitary; increases water reabsorption in collecting ducts.

options.text: Water, ions, glucose, amino acids, urea — NOT proteins or blood cells

quiz_questions.question_text: The PCT reabsorbs approximately _____ of the filtrate.
More than _____ of the filtrate is reabsorbed back into the blood.
Glucose is normally completely reabsorbed in the:
quiz_questions.explanation: All glucose reabsorbed unless blood glucose is very high (diabetes).

quiz_questions.question_text: In uncontrolled diabetes, glucose appears in urine because:

options.text: Blood glucose exceeds the renal threshold; transporters are saturated

quiz_questions.question_text: The descending limb of the loop of Henle is permeable to:
The ascending limb of the loop of Henle:

options.text: Actively pumps out NaCl but is impermeable to water

quiz_questions.explanation: Dilutes the filtrate; concentrates the medulla.

quiz_questions.question_text: Aldosterone acts on the DCT and collecting duct to:

options.text: Reabsorb Na⁺ (and water follows) and secrete K⁺
Inserting aquaporin channels in the collecting duct
Blood pressure (or NaCl) is low → activates RAAS
Angiotensin I to angiotensin II (in the lungs)

quiz_questions.explanation: ACE inhibitors are used to treat hypertension.

options.text: Vasoconstriction + aldosterone release + ADH release → ↑ BP

quiz_questions.question_text: ANP (atrial natriuretic peptide) opposes RAAS by:

options.text: Promoting Na⁺ and water excretion → ↓ blood volume and BP

quiz_questions.question_text: Creatinine clearance is used to estimate:
A normal urine output is approximately:

quiz_questions.explanation: Sign of kidney dysfunction or dehydration.

options.text: Promoting urine output → reducing blood volume → lowering BP

quiz_questions.explanation: Various classes target different nephron segments.

quiz_questions.question_text: Understanding kidney function is critical for:

options.text: Managing hypertension, diabetes, kidney disease, electrolyte disorders, and drug dosing

quiz_questions.explanation: Kidneys affect nearly every organ system.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Fluid & Electrolyte Balance
summary_sections.content_html:
Fluid & Electrolyte Balance

Body Fluid Compartments

Total body water ~60% of body weight. ICF (intracellular fluid) ~2/3; ECF (extracellular fluid) ~1/3 (plasma + interstitial).

Key Electrolytes

  • Na⁺: Major ECF cation; regulates osmolarity and fluid volume.
  • K⁺: Major ICF cation; essential for membrane potential, cardiac function.
  • Ca²⁺: Muscle contraction, nerve signaling, clotting, bone.
  • Acid-base: pH 7.35–7.45. Buffers (bicarbonate), lungs (CO₂), kidneys (H⁺/HCO₃⁻).

flashcards.definition: Intracellular fluid; ~2/3 of total body water; K⁺ is the major cation.
Extracellular fluid (~1/3 of TBW); includes plasma and interstitial fluid; Na⁺ is the major cation.
Abnormally high blood sodium; usually from water deficit → dehydration.
Low blood potassium; can cause dangerous cardiac arrhythmias.
Arterial pH < 7.35 due to HCO₃⁻ loss or acid gain; kidneys excrete H⁺, retain HCO₃⁻.

quiz_questions.question_text: Total body water is approximately _____ of body weight.
The intracellular fluid (ICF) compartment holds about:
The major cation of the ECF is:
The major cation of the ICF is:

options.text: Excess water intake, SIADH, or Na⁺ loss

quiz_questions.question_text: Hypokalemia is dangerous because it can cause:

options.text: It can cause fatal cardiac arrhythmias (tall T waves on ECG, cardiac arrest)
Muscle contraction, nerve signaling, blood clotting, and bone structure
Tetany (muscle spasms), numbness, Trousseau's and Chvostek's signs
H₂CO₃ ⇌ H⁺ + HCO₃⁻; most important ECF buffer
Diabetic ketoacidosis, renal failure, or severe diarrhea (loss of HCO₃⁻)
Prolonged vomiting (loss of HCl) or excess antacids

quiz_questions.question_text: Edema (fluid accumulation in tissues) can result from:

options.text: Low albumin (decreased oncotic pressure), heart failure, or lymphatic obstruction

quiz_questions.question_text: IV fluid therapy (e.g., normal saline, lactated Ringer's) is used to:

options.text: Restore fluid volume, correct electrolyte imbalances, and maintain hydration

quiz_questions.question_text: Understanding fluid and electrolyte balance is essential for:

options.text: Managing dehydration, shock, heart failure, kidney disease, and nearly all medical conditions

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Male Reproductive System
summary_sections.content_html:
Male Reproductive System

Anatomy

  • Testes: Produce sperm (seminiferous tubules) and testosterone (Leydig cells). Located in scrotum (cooler temperature).
  • Epididymis: Sperm maturation and storage.
  • Vas deferens → ejaculatory duct → urethra: Sperm transport pathway.
  • Accessory glands: Seminal vesicles (fructose, prostaglandins), prostate (alkaline fluid), bulbourethral glands (pre-ejaculate).

Spermatogenesis

Spermatogonia (2n) → primary spermatocyte → meiosis I → secondary → meiosis II → spermatids → spermatozoa. ~74 days. FSH + testosterone drive the process.

flashcards.definition: Formation of sperm from spermatogonia through meiosis; occurs in seminiferous tubules (~74 days).
Interstitial cells that produce testosterone in response to LH.
'Nurse cells' in seminiferous tubules; support, nourish, and protect developing sperm; form blood-testis barrier.
Primary male sex hormone; produced by Leydig cells; drives secondary sex characteristics, spermatogenesis, and libido.
Muscular duct that transports sperm from the epididymis to the ejaculatory duct.

options.text: Support, nourish, and protect developing sperm; form the blood-testis barrier

quiz_questions.question_text: The scrotum keeps the testes at a temperature that is:

options.text: Slightly lower than core body temperature (~2–3°C below)

quiz_questions.question_text: Sperm mature and are stored in the:

options.text: Cutting and sealing the vas deferens (male sterilization)
~60-70% of semen volume (fructose for energy, prostaglandins)
Alkaline fluid (neutralizes vaginal acidity, contains enzymes)

quiz_questions.explanation: Protects sperm in the vaginal environment.

options.text: Is non-cancerous prostate enlargement — common in older men → urinary obstruction
One of the most common cancers in men; screened with PSA test
Secondary sex characteristics (facial hair, deep voice, muscle mass), spermatogenesis, and libido

quiz_questions.question_text: Erectile dysfunction (ED) can be caused by:

options.text: Vascular disease, diabetes, neurological disorders, medications, or psychological factors
Inhibits FSH release (negative feedback to the pituitary)
Undescended testis (failure to descend into scrotum); increases cancer risk if untreated

quiz_questions.question_text: Understanding male reproductive anatomy is essential for:

options.text: Diagnosing infertility, BPH, prostate cancer, STIs, and hormonal disorders

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Female Reproductive System
summary_sections.content_html:
Female Reproductive System

Anatomy

  • Ovaries: Produce oocytes and hormones (estrogen, progesterone). Oogenesis begins prenatally.
  • Uterine (fallopian) tubes: Site of fertilization; fimbriae sweep oocyte in.
  • Uterus: Endometrium (inner lining, sheds during menstruation), myometrium (smooth muscle).
  • Vagina: Birth canal, receives penis.

Menstrual Cycle (~28 days)

Follicular phase (FSH → follicle growth → estrogen ↑) → LH surge → ovulation → luteal phase (corpus luteum → progesterone ↑) → if no fertilization → corpus luteum degenerates → menstruation.

flashcards.definition: Formation of oocytes; begins prenatally, arrested at prophase I; resumes at puberty with each cycle.
Release of a secondary oocyte from the ovary at mid-cycle (~day 14), triggered by LH surge.
Structure formed from the ruptured follicle post-ovulation; secretes progesterone and estrogen.
Inner lining of the uterus; thickens each cycle for implantation; sheds during menstruation.
Primary female hormone; promotes endometrial growth, secondary sex characteristics, and bone density.

options.text: Prenatally (before birth); arrested at prophase I until puberty

quiz_questions.explanation: Females are born with all primary oocytes.
Sperm meets egg in the tube.

options.text: Are finger-like projections that sweep the released oocyte into the fallopian tube
Thickens under estrogen/progesterone influence; sheds if implantation doesn't occur (menstruation)
The thick smooth muscle layer of the uterus (contracts during labor)

quiz_questions.question_text: The follicular phase is dominated by:

options.text: FSH and estrogen (follicle growth and endometrial proliferation)
A surge in LH (and FSH) at mid-cycle
The corpus luteum degenerates → progesterone drops → menstruation

quiz_questions.question_text: hCG (human chorionic gonadotropin) in early pregnancy:

options.text: Maintains the corpus luteum (preventing menstruation) until the placenta takes over

quiz_questions.explanation: hCG is the basis of pregnancy tests.

options.text: Endometrial growth, secondary sex characteristics (breast development), and bone density
Prepare and maintain the endometrium for implantation; maintain pregnancy
Is a common hormonal disorder causing irregular cycles, excess androgens, and ovarian cysts

quiz_questions.explanation: Affects ~10% of women of reproductive age.

options.text: Growth of endometrial tissue outside the uterus → pain, infertility

quiz_questions.explanation: Can implant on ovaries, tubes, peritoneum.

options.text: Precancerous or cancerous cells on the cervix (often caused by HPV)
The permanent cessation of menstruation (average age ~51); ovarian function declines
The embryo implants outside the uterus (most commonly in the fallopian tube)
Suppressing ovulation (inhibiting FSH/LH surge via synthetic estrogen and progesterone)

quiz_questions.explanation: Multiple mechanisms; ovulation suppression is primary.

options.text: Fertilizing oocytes with sperm in a lab, then transferring embryos to the uterus

quiz_questions.question_text: Understanding female reproductive anatomy is essential for:

options.text: Managing menstrual disorders, infertility, pregnancy, contraception, and gynecological cancers

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Hormonal Regulation of Reproduction
summary_sections.content_html:
Hormonal Regulation of Reproduction

Hypothalamic-Pituitary-Gonadal (HPG) Axis

GnRH (hypothalamus) → FSH + LH (anterior pituitary) → Gonads (testes/ovaries) → sex hormones → negative feedback to hypothalamus/pituitary.

Males

GnRH → LH → testosterone (Leydig); GnRH → FSH → spermatogenesis (Sertoli). Testosterone + inhibin = negative feedback.

Females

GnRH → FSH → follicle growth → estrogen. Mid-cycle: estrogen surge → positive feedback → LH surge → ovulation. Luteal: progesterone → negative feedback.

flashcards.definition: Gonadotropin-releasing hormone from the hypothalamus; stimulates FSH and LH release.
Follicle-stimulating hormone; stimulates follicle growth (females) or spermatogenesis (males).
Luteinizing hormone; triggers ovulation (females) or testosterone production (males).
Hypothalamic-Pituitary-Gonadal axis; the hormonal control system for reproduction.
High estrogen in the late follicular phase triggers a positive feedback loop → LH surge → ovulation.

quiz_questions.question_text: GnRH stimulates the anterior pituitary to release:

options.text: Both the hypothalamus and anterior pituitary (↓ GnRH, FSH, LH)
Ovarian follicle growth and estrogen production

quiz_questions.question_text: The LH surge is triggered by:

options.text: High estrogen levels in the late follicular phase (positive feedback)

quiz_questions.question_text: After ovulation, the corpus luteum produces:
If pregnancy occurs, _____ maintains the corpus luteum.

options.text: hCG (human chorionic gonadotropin from the embryo)

quiz_questions.question_text: The placenta eventually takes over hormone production around:
Oral contraceptives containing estrogen/progesterone work by:
quiz_questions.explanation: Hormonal suppression of the HPG axis.

options.text: Blocking estrogen receptors at the hypothalamus/pituitary → ↑ FSH/LH → stimulates ovulation

quiz_questions.explanation: Tricks the brain into making more gonadotropins.

options.text: Selectively inhibits FSH release from the anterior pituitary

quiz_questions.question_text: GnRH is released in a _____ pattern.

options.text: Pulsatile (intermittent pulses are essential for normal FSH/LH secretion)
Increased GnRH pulsatile secretion from the hypothalamus
Abnormally early onset of puberty (before age 8 in girls, 9 in boys)
Underactive gonads → low sex hormones → delayed puberty, infertility

quiz_questions.question_text: Testosterone replacement therapy may be used for:

options.text: Men with hypogonadism (low testosterone with symptoms)

quiz_questions.question_text: The HPG axis has clinical relevance in:

options.text: Infertility, contraception, puberty disorders, menopause, and hormonal cancers

quiz_questions.question_text: Understanding hormonal regulation is essential for:

options.text: Managing reproductive health, fertility treatments, contraception, and hormonal disorders

u9_l9.7.title: Disorders of the Urinary & Reproductive Systems

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Disorders of the Urinary & Reproductive Systems
summary_sections.content_html:
Disorders of the Urinary & Reproductive Systems

Urinary Disorders

  • UTI: Bacterial infection (often E. coli); more common in females (shorter urethra).
  • Kidney stones (nephrolithiasis): Calcium oxalate most common. Severe flank pain (renal colic).
  • Acute kidney injury (AKI): Sudden loss of function (prerenal, intrarenal, postrenal). Oliguria, ↑ creatinine/BUN.
  • Chronic kidney disease (CKD): Progressive loss of nephrons; stages 1-5; stage 5 = end-stage → dialysis or transplant.

Reproductive Disorders

  • STIs: Chlamydia, gonorrhea, syphilis, HPV, HIV, herpes.
  • Infertility: ~15% of couples; multifactorial.

flashcards.definition: Urinary tract infection; bacterial (commonly E. coli); presents with dysuria, frequency, urgency.
Kidney stones; calcium oxalate most common; cause severe renal colic.
Chronic kidney disease; progressive nephron loss; Stage 5 (GFR <15) requires dialysis or transplant.
Treatment that filters blood when kidneys fail; hemodialysis or peritoneal dialysis.
Sexually transmitted infection; includes chlamydia, gonorrhea, syphilis, HPV, HIV, herpes.

quiz_questions.question_text: The most common cause of UTIs is:
quiz_questions.explanation: E. coli from GI tract ascends the urethra.

quiz_questions.question_text: UTIs are more common in females because:

options.text: The female urethra is shorter (~4 cm), allowing easier bacterial ascent
Dysuria (painful urination), frequency, urgency, and sometimes hematuria

quiz_questions.question_text: The most common type of kidney stone is:
Renal colic (kidney stone pain) is typically:

options.text: Severe, colicky flank pain that may radiate to the groin

quiz_questions.explanation: One of the most severe pains known.

options.text: Adequate hydration, dietary modifications (reduce oxalate, moderate calcium)

quiz_questions.explanation: Hydration is the most important preventive measure.

quiz_questions.question_text: AKI (acute kidney injury) is characterized by:

options.text: Sudden decline in kidney function (↑ creatinine, ↑ BUN, ± oliguria)
End-stage renal disease (ESRD) — requires dialysis or transplant
Filtering the patient's blood through an external dialyzer machine
Inflammation of the glomeruli (can be caused by infections, autoimmunity)
The most common bacterial STI; often asymptomatic; can cause PID and infertility if untreated
Certain strains cause cervical, oropharyngeal, and other cancers; vaccine available
CD4+ T-helper cells (weakening the immune system)
A surgical emergency: twisting of the spermatic cord → ischemia → requires urgent surgery

quiz_questions.explanation: Must be treated within hours to save the testis.

options.text: A genetic disorder causing multiple fluid-filled cysts in the kidneys → progressive renal failure

quiz_questions.explanation: Autosomal dominant (most common genetic kidney disease).

options.text: Stress (sneezing/coughing), urge (overactive bladder), overflow, and functional
A kidney infection (upper UTI); presents with fever, flank pain, and CVA tenderness

quiz_questions.explanation: Both male and female factors involved.

quiz_questions.question_text: Understanding these disorders is critical because:

options.text: Urinary and reproductive conditions are among the most common and impactful health issues globally

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: AP Prep: Renal Physiology
summary_sections.content_html:
AP Prep: Renal Physiology

Exam Focus Areas

  • Nephron structure and function (filtration, reabsorption, secretion).
  • GFR regulation: myogenic mechanism, tubuloglomerular feedback, RAAS.
  • Countercurrent multiplier and concentrating mechanism.
  • Hormonal regulation: ADH, aldosterone, ANP, PTH.
  • Acid-base balance: respiratory vs. metabolic; compensation.
  • Urinalysis interpretation.

flashcards.definition: Loop of Henle mechanism creating a medullary concentration gradient for water reabsorption.
Intrinsic renal autoregulation: afferent arteriole constricts when stretched by high BP to maintain constant GFR.
Macula densa senses NaCl in DCT; signals JGA to adjust afferent arteriole tone and renin release.
Volume of plasma completely cleared of a substance per unit time; used to estimate GFR.
Laboratory analysis of urine; screens for glucose, protein, blood, bacteria, pH, and specific gravity.

quiz_questions.question_text: The countercurrent multiplier operates in the:

options.text: Loop of Henle (descending and ascending limbs)

quiz_questions.question_text: The descending limb of the loop of Henle is permeable to _____ but not _____.

options.text: Water; NaCl (water leaves, concentrating the tubular fluid)

quiz_questions.question_text: The thick ascending limb actively pumps out:

options.text: NaCl (via the Na⁺/K⁺/2Cl⁻ cotransporter) — impermeable to water

quiz_questions.explanation: Creates dilute tubular fluid and hypertonic medulla.

options.text: Na⁺/K⁺/2Cl⁻ cotransporter in the thick ascending limb

quiz_questions.question_text: ADH acts on the _____ to increase water reabsorption.

options.text: Na⁺ reabsorption (and K⁺/H⁺ secretion) in the DCT/collecting duct

quiz_questions.question_text: ANP (atrial natriuretic peptide) is released when:

options.text: Atrial walls are stretched (volume overload) → promotes Na⁺ and water excretion
Increasing Ca²⁺ reabsorption in the DCT and activating vitamin D
GFR accurately (freely filtered, not reabsorbed or secreted)
Renal plasma flow (RPF); PAH is filtered AND secreted

quiz_questions.question_text: Plasma creatinine is inversely related to:

options.text: Renal failure, dehydration, high protein diet, and GI bleeding

quiz_questions.question_text: Respiratory compensation for metabolic acidosis involves:

options.text: Hyperventilation (to blow off CO₂ and raise pH)

quiz_questions.question_text: Renal compensation for respiratory acidosis involves:

options.text: Glomerular damage (allowing protein to leak into filtrate)

quiz_questions.question_text: Glycosuria (glucose in urine) in the absence of diabetes may indicate:

options.text: Renal tubular defects (failure to reabsorb glucose) or very high blood glucose

quiz_questions.question_text: For the AP exam, understand the sequence:
quiz_questions.explanation: The three steps of urine formation.

options.text: Cardiovascular (BP), endocrine (hormones), respiratory (acid-base), and nervous systems

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Immune System Overview
summary_sections.content_html:
Immune System Overview

Innate (Non-Specific) Immunity

  • First line: Skin, mucous membranes, secretions (lysozyme, HCl, sebum).
  • Second line: Phagocytes (neutrophils, macrophages), NK cells, inflammation, fever, complement, interferons.

Adaptive (Specific) Immunity

  • Humoral: B cells → plasma cells → antibodies (IgG, IgA, IgM, IgE, IgD).
  • Cell-mediated: T cells — CD4⁺ helper (coordinate response), CD8⁺ cytotoxic (kill infected cells).
  • Memory cells: Long-lived B and T cells for rapid secondary response.

flashcards.definition: Non-specific, rapid defense present from birth; includes barriers, phagocytes, inflammation, and complement.
Specific, slower initial response; involves B cells (antibodies) and T cells; creates immunological memory.
Protein produced by plasma cells that recognizes and binds specific antigens; IgG, IgA, IgM, IgE, IgD.
Coordinates adaptive immune responses; activates B cells and CD8⁺ T cells; targeted by HIV.
Long-lived lymphocyte that enables faster, stronger immune response upon re-exposure to an antigen.

quiz_questions.question_text: The first line of defense includes:

options.text: Skin, mucous membranes, and secretions (lysozyme, acid, sebum)
The most abundant WBCs; first responders to infection (phagocytes)
Phagocytes AND antigen-presenting cells (bridge innate and adaptive immunity)
Redness, heat, swelling, and pain (rubor, calor, tumor, dolor)
Is a cascade of ~30 proteins that enhances phagocytosis, causes lysis, and promotes inflammation
The most abundant antibody in blood; crosses the placenta; provides passive immunity to the fetus
Allergic reactions and parasitic infections (binds mast cells → histamine release)
Coordinate the immune response by activating B cells, CD8⁺ T cells, and macrophages
Directly kill virus-infected cells and cancer cells (via perforin/granzymes)
Exposing the immune system to antigens (weakened/inactivated pathogen or antigen) → memory cells formed
The immune system attacks the body's own tissues (loss of self-tolerance)

quiz_questions.explanation: Examples: lupus, RA, MS, type 1 diabetes.

options.text: Primary (genetic, e.g., SCID) or secondary (acquired, e.g., HIV/AIDS)

quiz_questions.question_text: The lymphatic system's role in immunity includes:

options.text: Filtering lymph (lymph nodes), housing immune cells, and transporting antigens to lymphoid organs

quiz_questions.explanation: Lymph nodes are immune surveillance hubs.

options.text: Are proteins released by virus-infected cells that signal neighboring cells to increase antiviral defenses
Are only produced by T cells

quiz_questions.question_text: Understanding the immune system is essential for:

options.text: Managing infections, autoimmune diseases, allergies, transplant rejection, cancer immunotherapy, and vaccination

quiz_questions.explanation: One of the most important body systems.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Interactions Between Organ Systems
summary_sections.content_html:
Interactions Between Organ Systems

Homeostasis Through Integration

No organ system works in isolation. Maintaining homeostasis requires coordinated communication between all 11 organ systems.

Key Interactions

  • Exercise response: Muscular (contraction) + skeletal (support) + nervous (motor commands) + cardiovascular (↑ CO) + respiratory (↑ ventilation) + endocrine (epinephrine, cortisol).
  • Eating a meal: Digestive (digestion/absorption) + nervous (vagal stimulation) + endocrine (insulin, GI hormones) + cardiovascular (nutrient transport) + urinary (waste excretion).
  • Hemorrhage response: Cardiovascular (↑ HR, vasoconstriction) + nervous (baroreceptor reflex) + endocrine (RAAS, ADH) + urinary (↓ urine output) + immune (clotting).

flashcards.definition: Maintenance of a stable internal environment through coordinated organ system activity.
Volume of blood pumped by the heart per minute (HR × SV); increases during exercise.
Autonomic reflex that adjusts heart rate and vascular tone in response to blood pressure changes.
Pancreatic hormone that lowers blood glucose by promoting cellular uptake and glycogen storage.
Adrenal medulla hormone that increases HR, BP, bronchodilation, and glucose — 'fight-or-flight.'

quiz_questions.question_text: During exercise, cardiac output increases because:

options.text: Both heart rate AND stroke volume increase to meet muscle oxygen demand

quiz_questions.question_text: The respiratory system responds to exercise by:

options.text: Increasing ventilation rate and depth (to supply more O₂ and remove CO₂)
Increases HR, BP, bronchodilation, and mobilizes glucose from glycogen

quiz_questions.question_text: After eating, the parasympathetic nervous system:

options.text: Stimulates digestion ('rest and digest' — increases GI secretion and motility)
Lowers blood glucose by promoting cellular uptake, glycogenesis, and lipogenesis
Decreased blood pressure → trigger sympathetic response (↑ HR, vasoconstriction)

quiz_questions.explanation: Baroreceptor reflex compensates for blood loss.

quiz_questions.question_text: RAAS activation during hemorrhage leads to:

options.text: Na⁺ and water retention + vasoconstriction → restores blood volume and pressure

quiz_questions.question_text: The integumentary system (skin) interacts with other systems by:

options.text: Protecting all systems (barrier), synthesizing vitamin D (skeletal), thermoregulation (cardiovascular), and sensory input (nervous)

quiz_questions.question_text: The skeletal system supports other systems by:

options.text: Providing structure, protecting organs (brain, heart, lungs), producing blood cells (marrow), and storing minerals (Ca²⁺)
Coordinated response: immune (pyrogens) + nervous (hypothalamic thermostat) + cardiovascular (vasodilation) + muscular (shivering)
Reproductive, endocrine, cardiovascular, respiratory, urinary, musculoskeletal, immune, and digestive systems

quiz_questions.question_text: The nervous and endocrine systems work together by:

options.text: The nervous system triggers rapid responses while the endocrine system sustains them (e.g., fight-or-flight)
Only one functioning at a time

quiz_questions.question_text: Blood serves as a communication highway by:

options.text: Transporting O₂, CO₂, nutrients, hormones, waste, immune cells, and heat
Cardiovascular (↓ blood volume), nervous (confusion), renal (↓ GFR), muscular (cramps), and thermoregulatory
Endocrine (insulin deficit) → cardiovascular (atherosclerosis), nervous (neuropathy), renal (nephropathy), visual (retinopathy), immune (infections)
Nervous (HPA axis), endocrine (cortisol), cardiovascular (hypertension), immune (suppression), digestive (ulcers), and mental health
Gradual decline in all organ systems: decreased GFR, cardiac output, bone density, immune function, etc.

quiz_questions.question_text: A systems approach to medicine means:

options.text: Treating one organ at a time
Considering how all body systems interact when diagnosing and treating disease
Most diseases affect multiple organ systems, and treatment must consider the whole patient

quiz_questions.question_text: Studying organ system interactions prepares students for:

options.text: Understanding complex clinical scenarios, practicing systems-based medicine, and succeeding on AP exams

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Case Studies in Pathophysiology
summary_sections.content_html:
Case Studies in Pathophysiology

Case 1: Congestive Heart Failure (CHF)

68 y/o with dyspnea, edema, elevated JVP. Chest X-ray: pulmonary congestion. Echo: reduced EF. CHF involves cardiovascular (pump failure), respiratory (pulmonary edema), renal (fluid retention), and endocrine (RAAS activation) systems.

Case 2: Type 1 Diabetes (DKA)

16 y/o with polyuria, polydipsia, weight loss, fruity breath. Labs: glucose 450, pH 7.1, ketones +++. Endocrine (no insulin), renal (glucosuria, osmotic diuresis), respiratory (Kussmaul breathing), and nervous system (altered mental status) involvement.

Case 3: Sepsis

45 y/o post-surgical with fever, tachycardia, hypotension, altered mental status. Blood cultures positive. Immune (overwhelming infection), cardiovascular (vasodilation, hypotension), renal (AKI), respiratory (ARDS), and coagulation (DIC) involved.

flashcards.definition: Congestive heart failure; the heart can't pump effectively → fluid backs up into lungs and tissues.
Percentage of blood ejected from the ventricle per beat; normal ~55-70%; reduced in heart failure.
Diabetic ketoacidosis; life-threatening complication of type 1 diabetes: hyperglycemia + acidosis + ketones.
Life-threatening organ dysfunction caused by a dysregulated host response to infection.
Acute respiratory distress syndrome; severe lung inflammation/fluid accumulation → respiratory failure.

options.text: Cannot pump effectively enough to meet the body's demands

quiz_questions.question_text: Pulmonary edema in left-sided heart failure occurs because:

options.text: Blood backs up into pulmonary veins → fluid leaks into alveoli

quiz_questions.question_text: In CHF, the kidneys respond by:

options.text: Retaining Na⁺ and water (RAAS activation) → worsening fluid overload
Blocking angiotensin II → vasodilation + reduced preload/afterload + less aldosterone

quiz_questions.question_text: In DKA, the body uses _____ for energy because insulin is absent.

options.text: Fat (fatty acid oxidation → ketone bodies → acidosis)
Deep, rapid breathing to blow off CO₂ (respiratory compensation for metabolic acidosis)
IV insulin, IV fluids, electrolyte replacement (especially K⁺), and monitoring

quiz_questions.explanation: Must correct glucose, volume, and electrolytes.

options.text: Hypotension (distributive shock) — despite high cardiac output initially

quiz_questions.question_text: Sepsis can lead to multi-organ failure because:

options.text: Systemic inflammation damages multiple organs: kidneys (AKI), lungs (ARDS), liver, brain

quiz_questions.question_text: DIC (disseminated intravascular coagulation) in sepsis causes:

options.text: Simultaneous excessive clotting AND bleeding (consumption of clotting factors)
IV fluids, blood cultures, antibiotics, and lactate measurement — all within 1 hour
Cardiovascular (coronary occlusion), nervous (pain), endocrine (stress hormones), and potentially renal and respiratory systems
Nervous (focal deficits), cardiovascular (cause: embolism or hemorrhage), musculoskeletal (weakness), and respiratory (airway compromise) system involvement
Respiratory (airflow obstruction), cardiovascular (cor pulmonale), musculoskeletal (cachexia), and mental health

quiz_questions.question_text: A patient with liver cirrhosis may present with:

options.text: Jaundice, ascites, coagulopathy, portal hypertension, encephalopathy, and renal failure (hepatorenal syndrome)
Integrating knowledge of anatomy, physiology, and multiple organ system interactions
Most serious diseases involve multiple organ systems and require a systems-based approach

quiz_questions.question_text: The best approach to these cases on an AP exam is:

options.text: Systematically identifying which organ systems are involved and explaining the pathophysiology of each
Connecting cause → mechanism → effect across organ systems
Clinical reasoning, AP exam scenarios, and future healthcare careers

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Review of Key Concepts
summary_sections.content_html:
Review of Key Concepts

By Organ System

  • Skeletal: Support, protection, movement, hematopoiesis, mineral storage.
  • Muscular: Movement, posture, heat production. Sliding filament theory.
  • Nervous: Rapid communication via action potentials. CNS + PNS.
  • Endocrine: Hormonal regulation — feedback loops, HPG/HPA axes.
  • Cardiovascular: Transport of blood, O₂, nutrients. Cardiac cycle.
  • Respiratory: Gas exchange. Ventilation mechanics.
  • Digestive: Mechanical/chemical digestion, absorption. Liver, pancreas.
  • Urinary: Filtration, reabsorption, secretion. Fluid/electrolyte/acid-base balance.
  • Reproductive: Gametogenesis, hormonal regulation (HPG axis), pregnancy.
  • Immune: Innate + adaptive immunity. Memory.

flashcards.definition: Muscle contraction mechanism: actin filaments slide past myosin; powered by ATP, regulated by Ca²⁺.
Rapid electrical signal in neurons/muscles: depolarization (Na⁺ in) → repolarization (K⁺ out).
One complete heartbeat: atrial systole → ventricular systole → diastole.
Homeostatic mechanism where the response opposes the stimulus (e.g., thermoregulation).
Passive, pressure-driven movement of fluid from glomerular capillaries into Bowman's capsule.

quiz_questions.question_text: The five functions of the skeletal system include:

options.text: Support, protection, movement, hematopoiesis, and mineral storage
How actin and myosin filaments interact to produce muscle contraction
Depolarization (Na⁺ influx) followed by repolarization (K⁺ efflux)
The response opposes the stimulus to restore a set point

quiz_questions.question_text: Positive feedback differs from negative in that:

options.text: The response amplifies the stimulus (e.g., oxytocin in labor, LH surge at ovulation)
Atrial systole → ventricular systole → diastole (one complete heartbeat)

quiz_questions.question_text: The three processes of urine formation are:
The menstrual cycle involves which key hormones?
quiz_questions.explanation: Complete hormonal regulation of the cycle.

quiz_questions.question_text: Homeostasis is maintained by all organ systems working:
The SA node is the heart's natural pacemaker located in the:

options.text: Breathing (contraction creates negative intrathoracic pressure → air inhalation)
Temperature, thirst, hunger, circadian rhythms, and endocrine function via the pituitary
Oxygen transport (each molecule carries up to 4 O₂)

quiz_questions.question_text: Insulin and glucagon have _____ effects on blood glucose.
The liver's role in metabolism includes:

options.text: Detoxification, bile production, protein synthesis, glycogen storage/release, and bilirubin processing

quiz_questions.question_text: The lymphatic system's dual role is:

options.text: Fluid return to blood AND immune defense (lymph nodes filter pathogens)

quiz_questions.question_text: Understanding all systems together is important because:

options.text: Clinical disease almost always involves multiple systems, and effective treatment requires whole-body understanding

quiz_questions.question_text: To prepare for the AP exam, students should:

options.text: Understand mechanisms, integration, and be able to apply to clinical scenarios

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: AP Practice: Multi-System Questions
summary_sections.content_html:
AP Practice: Multi-System Questions

This lesson focuses on higher-order AP-style questions that integrate knowledge from multiple organ systems. Practice identifying connections across anatomy and physiology to strengthen exam readiness.

Strategy

  • Read the clinical scenario carefully.
  • Identify ALL organ systems involved.
  • Think about cause → mechanism → effect.
  • Eliminate obviously wrong answers first.

flashcards.definition: Systematic approach: gather data → identify problems → hypothesize → test → conclude.
Recognizing that most physiological events and diseases involve interactions among multiple organ systems.
Body's automatic adjustments to maintain homeostasis when one system is stressed or failing.
Study of how disease disrupts normal physiology; connects anatomy to clinical medicine.
Process of listing and narrowing possible diagnoses based on signs, symptoms, and test results.

quiz_questions.question_text: A marathon runner collapses. Which systems are involved?

options.text: Cardiovascular (dehydration ↓ CO), thermoregulatory (hyperthermia), renal (↓ GFR), musculoskeletal, nervous (confusion), and endocrine

quiz_questions.question_text: In anaphylaxis, which systems are affected?

options.text: Immune (IgE/mast cells → histamine), cardiovascular (vasodilation → shock), respiratory (bronchospasm → airway obstruction), skin (urticaria)

quiz_questions.question_text: A patient with chronic kidney disease develops anemia because:

options.text: Kidneys produce less erythropoietin (EPO) → ↓ RBC production
Elevated blood calcium (increased bone resorption, renal Ca²⁺ reabsorption, and intestinal absorption via vitamin D)

quiz_questions.explanation: PTH affects skeletal, renal, and GI systems.

quiz_questions.question_text: A patient with a pulmonary embolism (PE) develops:

options.text: Respiratory failure (V/Q mismatch), cardiovascular compromise (right heart strain), and potentially hemodynamic collapse
Metabolism (weight gain), cardiovascular (bradycardia), nervous (cognitive slowing), musculoskeletal (weakness), integumentary (cold intolerance, dry skin)

quiz_questions.explanation: Thyroid hormones affect every organ system.

quiz_questions.question_text: A spinal cord injury at C3 can cause:

options.text: Loss of voluntary movement below injury, respiratory failure (phrenic nerve to diaphragm at C3-5), autonomic dysreflexia, neurogenic bladder
Cardiovascular (anemia), skeletal (bone crisis), splenic (autosplenectomy), renal, pulmonary, and neurological systems

quiz_questions.explanation: Systemic disease from one genetic mutation.

quiz_questions.question_text: A patient taking corticosteroids chronically may develop:

options.text: Cushing-like features: immunosuppression, osteoporosis, hyperglycemia, muscle wasting, poor wound healing, adrenal suppression

quiz_questions.question_text: Pregnancy affects the cardiovascular system by:

options.text: Increasing blood volume (~50%), cardiac output (~40%), and heart rate
Chronic hyperglycemia damages glomerular capillaries → proteinuria → progressive renal failure

quiz_questions.question_text: Liver failure can cause cerebral edema because:

options.text: Ammonia accumulates (liver can't convert to urea) → crosses BBB → hepatic encephalopathy

quiz_questions.question_text: A patient with COPD develops right heart failure (cor pulmonale) because:

options.text: Chronic hypoxic pulmonary vasoconstriction → pulmonary hypertension → right ventricle overload
Integumentary (barrier loss), immune (infection risk), cardiovascular (fluid loss → shock), renal (↓ perfusion), metabolic (hypermetabolic state)
Cardiovascular disease, type 2 diabetes, joint disease, sleep apnea, certain cancers, and more
Think about cause → mechanism → effect and identify every system involved

quiz_questions.question_text: Which is the best strategy for elimination?

options.text: Eliminate answers that only mention one system when the scenario clearly involves multiple
Spend roughly equal time per question; flag difficult ones and return later
Spend all time on hard questions
They build familiarity with question format, test knowledge application, and identify weak areas

quiz_questions.question_text: The best AP exam preparation strategy involves:

options.text: Understanding mechanisms, practicing application questions, reviewing weak areas, and integrating across systems

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Capstone Project: Human Body Systems
summary_sections.content_html:
Capstone Project: Human Body Systems

Project Overview

Students select a clinical condition (e.g., heart attack, diabetes, sepsis) and analyze it from a multi-system perspective, documenting the pathophysiology across all affected organ systems.

Steps

  • 1. Choose a disease or case.
  • 2. Identify all organ systems affected.
  • 3. Explain the pathophysiology of each system's involvement.
  • 4. Describe how systems compensate or fail.
  • 5. Discuss treatment strategies that target each system.
  • 6. Present findings in a structured format (paper, poster, or presentation).

flashcards.definition: Culminating assignment that integrates knowledge from across all units into a comprehensive analysis.
Systematic explanation of how a disease disrupts normal physiology across organ systems.
Framework for understanding diseases by analyzing their effects on each organ system.
Using the best available research evidence to guide clinical decisions and treatments.
The signs, symptoms, and findings that a patient displays when presenting with a disease.

quiz_questions.question_text: A capstone project in anatomy should:

options.text: Integrate knowledge from multiple units and organ systems to analyze a clinical condition

quiz_questions.question_text: When selecting a disease for the capstone, a good choice is:

options.text: A condition that involves multiple organ systems (e.g., diabetes, heart failure, sepsis)
Choosing a disease and defining the scope of analysis
ALL organ systems affected by the chosen condition
Describing the mechanism of how the disease disrupts normal physiology in each system
Explaining how the body tries to maintain homeostasis (e.g., ↑ HR in hemorrhage, hyperventilation in acidosis)

quiz_questions.question_text: When systems fail to compensate, the result is:

options.text: Decompensation → organ failure → possible multi-organ dysfunction → death if untreated
Address each affected organ system and their interactions
Peer-reviewed journals, textbooks, clinical guidelines (e.g., UpToDate, PubMed)
Title, introduction, pathophysiology, multi-system analysis, treatment, conclusion, references, and visuals
Organized, clear, evidence-based, and demonstrate understanding of multi-system integration

quiz_questions.question_text: Peer review of capstone projects helps:

options.text: Identify gaps in reasoning, improve clarity, and strengthen the final product
Content accuracy, multi-system integration, evidence use, organization, and presentation quality

quiz_questions.question_text: Diabetes mellitus would involve analysis of:

options.text: Endocrine (insulin), cardiovascular (atherosclerosis), nervous (neuropathy), renal (nephropathy), visual (retinopathy), immune (infections)
Musculoskeletal (fractures), nervous (concussion/spinal cord), cardiovascular (hemorrhage), respiratory (pneumothorax), integumentary (lacerations)
Critical thinking, integration, research, analysis, communication, and clinical reasoning

quiz_questions.question_text: A strong capstone connects anatomy to:

options.text: Pathophysiology, clinical medicine, and real-world patient care
Plagiarism, superficial analysis, and ignoring system interactions

quiz_questions.question_text: The capstone project is valuable because it:

options.text: Synthesizes an entire course into a coherent, clinically relevant analysis
Future healthcare studies, AP exams, college-level coursework, and evidence-based practice

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Comprehensive Review & AP Prep
summary_sections.content_html:
Comprehensive Review & AP Prep

Final Review Checklist

  • ☐ Body organization, terminology, homeostasis (Unit 1).
  • ☐ Skeletal system: bone types, joints, disorders (Unit 2).
  • ☐ Muscular system: contraction, sliding filament (Unit 3).
  • ☐ Nervous system: action potentials, CNS/PNS, reflexes (Unit 4).
  • ☐ Endocrine system: hormones, feedback, glands (Unit 5).
  • ☐ Cardiovascular: heart, vessels, cardiac cycle, blood (Unit 6).
  • ☐ Respiratory: lungs, gas exchange, ventilation (Unit 7).
  • ☐ Digestive: organs, enzymes, absorption, liver (Unit 8).
  • ☐ Urinary & Reproductive: nephron, hormones, gametogenesis (Unit 9).
  • ☐ Integration: immune system, multi-system interactions (Unit 10).

Strategies: Focus on integration, practice multi-system questions, review weak areas, use active recall and spaced repetition.

flashcards.definition: Study technique: retrieve information from memory rather than passively re-reading; strengthens retention.
Reviewing material at increasing intervals to improve long-term retention.
Educational framework: Remember → Understand → Apply → Analyze → Evaluate → Create; AP tests higher levels.
The central theme of anatomy/physiology; all organ systems work to maintain a stable internal environment.
Using anatomical and physiological knowledge to understand, diagnose, and treat disease.
